September 4, 1998; 27 years ago (1998-09-04)[[a]](#cite_note-5) in [Menlo Park](/wiki/Menlo_Park,_California "Menlo Park, California"), [California](/wiki/California "California"), United States

Hennessy") ([Chairman](/wiki/Chair_(officer) "Chair (officer)")) * [Sundar Pichai](/wiki/Sundar_Pichai "Sundar Pichai") ([CEO](/wiki/Chief_executive_officer "Chief executive officer")) * [Ruth Porat](/wiki/Ruth_Porat "Ruth Porat") ([President](/wiki/President_(corporate_title) "President (corporate title)") and [CIO](/wiki/Chief_investment_officer "Chief investment officer")) * [Anat Ashkenazi](/wiki/Anat_Ashkenazi "Anat Ashkenazi") ([CFO](/wiki/Chief_financial_officer "Chief financial officer"))

* [Google Search](/wiki/Google_Search "Google Search") * [Android](/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)") * [Nest](/wiki/Google_Nest "Google Nest") * [Pixel](/wiki/Google_Pixel "Google Pixel") * [Workspace](/wiki/Google_Workspace "Google Workspace") * [Fitbit](/wiki/Fitbit "Fitbit") * [Waze](/wiki/Waze "Waze") * [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube") * [Gemini](/wiki/Gemini_(chatbot) "Gemini (chatbot)") * [Full list](/wiki/List_of_Google_products "List of Google products")

* [Adscape](/wiki/Adscape "Adscape") * [Cameyo](/wiki/Cameyo "Cameyo") * [Charleston Road Registry](/wiki/Charleston_Road_Registry "Charleston Road Registry") * [Endoxon](/wiki/Endoxon "Endoxon") * [FeedBurner](/wiki/FeedBurner "FeedBurner") * [ImageAmerica](/wiki/ImageAmerica "ImageAmerica") * [Kaltix](/wiki/Kaltix "Kaltix") * [Nest Labs](/wiki/Google_Nest "Google Nest") * [reCAPTCHA](/wiki/ReCAPTCHA "ReCAPTCHA") * [X Development](/wiki/X_Development "X Development") * [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube") * [ZipDash](/wiki/ZipDash "ZipDash")

([/ˈɡuː.ɡəl/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-us-googol.ogg "File:En-us-googol.ogg"), [*GOO-gəl*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is an American multinational technology corporation focused on [information technology](/wiki/Information_technology "Information technology"), [online advertising](/wiki/Online_advertising "Online advertising"), [search engine](/wiki/Search_engine "Search engine") technology, [email](/wiki/Email "Email"), [cloud computing](/wiki/Cloud_computing "Cloud computing"), software, [quantum computing](/wiki/Quantum_computing "Quantum computing"), [e-commerce](/wiki/E-commerce "E-commerce"), consumer electronics, and [artificial intelligence](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence "Artificial intelligence") (AI).[[9]](#cite_note-10) It has been referred to as "the most powerful company in the world" by the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC"),[[10]](#cite_note-11) and is one of the world's [most valuable brands](/wiki/List_of_most_valuable_brands "List of most valuable brands").[[11]](#cite_note-12)[[12]](#cite_note-13)[[13]](#cite_note-14) Google's parent company [Alphabet Inc.](/wiki/Alphabet_Inc. "Alphabet Inc.") has been described as a [Big Tech](/wiki/Big_Tech "Big Tech") company.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Talks at Google](/w/index.php?title=Talks_at_Google&redirect=no "Talks at Google"))

American multinational technology company

This article is about the company. For the search engine provided by the company, see [Google Search](/wiki/Google_Search "Google Search"). For the parent company with the stock tickers GOOG and GOOGL, see [Alphabet Inc.](/wiki/Alphabet_Inc. "Alphabet Inc.") For the number, see [Googol](/wiki/Googol "Googol"). For other uses, see [Google (disambiguation)](/wiki/Google_(disambiguation) "Google (disambiguation)").

**Google LLC** ([/ˈɡuː.ɡəl/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-us-googol.ogg "File:En-us-googol.ogg"), [*GOO-gəl*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is an American multinational technology corporation focused on [information technology](/wiki/Information_technology "Information technology"), [online advertising](/wiki/Online_advertising "Online advertising"), [search engine](/wiki/Search_engine "Search engine") technology, [email](/wiki/Email "Email"), [cloud computing](/wiki/Cloud_computing "Cloud computing"), software, [quantum computing](/wiki/Quantum_computing "Quantum computing"), [e-commerce](/wiki/E-commerce "E-commerce"), consumer electronics, and [artificial intelligence](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence "Artificial intelligence") (AI).[[9]](#cite_note-10) It has been referred to as "the most powerful company in the world" by the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC"),[[10]](#cite_note-11) and is one of the world's [most valuable brands](/wiki/List_of_most_valuable_brands "List of most valuable brands").[[11]](#cite_note-12)[[12]](#cite_note-13)[[13]](#cite_note-14) Google's parent company [Alphabet Inc.](/wiki/Alphabet_Inc. "Alphabet Inc.") has been described as a [Big Tech](/wiki/Big_Tech "Big Tech") company.

Google was founded in 1998 by American computer scientists [Larry Page](/wiki/Larry_Page "Larry Page") and [Sergey Brin](/wiki/Sergey_Brin "Sergey Brin"). Together, they own about 14% of its publicly listed shares and control 56% of its stockholder voting power through [super-voting stock](/wiki/Super-voting_stock "Super-voting stock"). The company went [public](/wiki/Public_company "Public company") via an [initial public offering](/wiki/Initial_public_offering "Initial public offering") (IPO) in 2004. In 2015, Google was reorganized as a wholly owned subsidiary of Alphabet Inc. Google is Alphabet's largest subsidiary and is a [holding company](/wiki/Holding_company "Holding company") for Alphabet's internet properties and interests. [Sundar Pichai](/wiki/Sundar_Pichai "Sundar Pichai") was appointed CEO of Google in 2015, replacing Larry Page, who became the CEO of Alphabet. In 2019, Pichai also became the CEO of Alphabet.[[14]](#cite_note-15)

After the success of its original service, [Google Search](/wiki/Google_Search "Google Search") (often known simply as "Google"), the company has rapidly grown to offer [a multitude of products](/wiki/List_of_Google_products "List of Google products") and [services](/wiki/List_of_Google_services "List of Google services"). These products address a wide range of use cases, including email ([Gmail](/wiki/Gmail "Gmail")), navigation and mapping ([Waze](/wiki/Waze "Waze"), [Maps](/wiki/Google_Maps "Google Maps"), and [Earth](/wiki/Google_Earth "Google Earth")), cloud computing ([Cloud](/wiki/Google_Cloud_Platform "Google Cloud Platform")), [web navigation](/wiki/Web_navigation "Web navigation") ([Chrome](/wiki/Google_Chrome "Google Chrome")), video sharing ([YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube")), productivity ([Workspace](/wiki/Google_Workspace "Google Workspace")), [operating systems](/wiki/Operating_system "Operating system") ([Android](/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)") and [ChromeOS](/wiki/ChromeOS "ChromeOS")), [cloud storage](/wiki/Cloud_storage "Cloud storage") ([Drive](/wiki/Google_Drive "Google Drive")), language translation ([Translate](/wiki/Google_Translate "Google Translate")), photo storage ([Photos](/wiki/Google_Photos "Google Photos")), [videotelephony](/wiki/Videotelephony "Videotelephony") ([Meet](/wiki/Google_Meet "Google Meet")), [smart home](/wiki/Home_automation "Home automation") ([Nest](/wiki/Google_Nest "Google Nest")), [smartphones](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone") ([Pixel](/wiki/Google_Pixel "Google Pixel")), [wearable technology](/wiki/Wearable_technology "Wearable technology") ([Pixel Watch](/wiki/Pixel_Watch "Pixel Watch") and [Fitbit](/wiki/Fitbit "Fitbit")), music streaming ([YouTube Music](/wiki/YouTube_Music "YouTube Music")), video on demand ([YouTube TV](/wiki/YouTube_TV "YouTube TV")), AI ([Google Assistant](/wiki/Google_Assistant "Google Assistant") and [Gemini](/wiki/Gemini_(chatbot) "Gemini (chatbot)")), [machine learning](/wiki/Machine_learning "Machine learning") APIs ([TensorFlow](/wiki/TensorFlow "TensorFlow")), AI chips ([TPU](/wiki/Tensor_Processing_Unit "Tensor Processing Unit")), and more. Many of these products and services are dominant in their respective industries, as is Google Search. [Discontinued Google products](/wiki/List_of_Google_products#Discontinued_products_and_services "List of Google products") include [gaming](/wiki/Video_game "Video game") ([Stadia](/wiki/Google_Stadia "Google Stadia")),[[15]](#cite_note-16) [Glass](/wiki/Google_Glass "Google Glass"), [Google+](/wiki/Google%2B "Google+"), [Reader](/wiki/Google_Reader "Google Reader"), [Play Music](/wiki/Google_Play_Music "Google Play Music"), [Nexus](/wiki/Google_Nexus "Google Nexus"), [Hangouts](/wiki/Google_Hangouts "Google Hangouts"), and [Inbox by Gmail](/wiki/Inbox_by_Gmail "Inbox by Gmail").[[16]](#cite_note-17)[[17]](#cite_note-18) Google's other ventures outside of internet services and consumer electronics include quantum computing ([Willow](/wiki/Willow_processor "Willow processor"), [Google Quantum AI](/wiki/Google_Quantum_AI "Google Quantum AI")), [self-driving cars](/wiki/Self-driving_car "Self-driving car") ([Waymo](/wiki/Waymo "Waymo")), and [transformer models](/wiki/Transformer_(deep_learning_architecture) "Transformer (deep learning architecture)") ([Google DeepMind](/wiki/Google_DeepMind "Google DeepMind")).[[18]](#cite_note-19)

Google Search and YouTube are the two [most-visited websites](/wiki/List_of_most-visited_websites "List of most-visited websites") worldwide, followed by [Facebook](/wiki/Facebook "Facebook"), [Instagram](/wiki/Instagram "Instagram"), and [ChatGPT](/wiki/ChatGPT "ChatGPT"). Google is the largest provider of search engines, mapping and navigation applications, [email services](/wiki/Mailbox_provider "Mailbox provider"), [office suites](/wiki/Productivity_software#Office_suite "Productivity software"), [online video platforms](/wiki/Online_video_platform "Online video platform"), photo and [cloud storage](/wiki/Cloud_storage "Cloud storage"), [mobile operating systems](/wiki/Mobile_operating_system "Mobile operating system"), [web browsers](/wiki/Web_browser "Web browser"), machine learning frameworks, and AI [virtual assistants](/wiki/Virtual_assistant "Virtual assistant") in the world as measured by market share.[[19]](#cite_note-20) On the list of the most valuable brands, Google is ranked second by *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")* as of January 2022,[[20]](#cite_note-21) and fourth by Interbrand as of February 2022.[[21]](#cite_note-22) [The company has received criticism](/wiki/Criticism_of_Google "Criticism of Google") involving issues such as [privacy concerns](/wiki/Privacy_concerns_with_Google "Privacy concerns with Google"), [tax avoidance](/wiki/Tax_avoidance_by_Google "Tax avoidance by Google"), [censorship](/wiki/Censorship_by_Google "Censorship by Google"), [search neutrality](/wiki/Search_neutrality "Search neutrality"), [antitrust](/wiki/Competition_law#United_States_antitrust "Competition law"), and abuse of its [monopoly](/wiki/Monopoly "Monopoly") position.[[22]](#cite_note-23)

## History

Main articles: [History of Google](/wiki/History_of_Google "History of Google") and [List of mergers and acquisitions by Alphabet](/wiki/List_of_mergers_and_acquisitions_by_Alphabet "List of mergers and acquisitions by Alphabet")

See also: [Alphabet Inc.](/wiki/Alphabet_Inc. "Alphabet Inc.")

### Early years

[![Larry Page and Sergey Brin](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Google_page_brin.jpg/250px-Google_page_brin.jpg)](/wiki/File:Google_page_brin.jpg)

[Larry Page](/wiki/Larry_Page "Larry Page") and [Sergey](/wiki/Sergey_Brin "Sergey Brin") [Brin](/wiki/Sergey_Brin "Sergey Brin") in 2003

Google began in January 1996 as a research project by [Larry Page](/wiki/Larry_Page "Larry Page") and [Sergey Brin](/wiki/Sergey_Brin "Sergey Brin") while they were both [PhD](/wiki/PhD "PhD") students at [Stanford University](/wiki/Stanford_University "Stanford University") in [California](/wiki/California "California"), United States.[[23]](#cite_note-howwestarted-24)[[24]](#cite_note-Coronabook-25)[[25]](#cite_note-milestones-26) The project initially involved an unofficial "third founder", [Scott Hassan](/wiki/Scott_Hassan "Scott Hassan"), the original lead programmer who wrote much of the code for the original Google Search engine, but he left before Google was officially founded as a company;[[26]](#cite_note-vanityfair-27)[[27]](#cite_note-28) Hassan went on to pursue a career in [robotics](/wiki/Robotics "Robotics") and founded the company [Willow Garage](/wiki/Willow_Garage "Willow Garage") in 2006.[[28]](#cite_note-29)[[29]](#cite_note-30)

[![Eric Schmidt, Sergey Brin, and Larry Page sitting together](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Schmidt-Brin-Page-20080520.jpg/250px-Schmidt-Brin-Page-20080520.jpg)](/wiki/File:Schmidt-Brin-Page-20080520.jpg)

Then chairman and CEO [Eric Schmidt](/wiki/Eric_Schmidt "Eric Schmidt") (left) with co-founders [Sergey Brin](/wiki/Sergey_Brin "Sergey Brin") (center) and [Larry Page](/wiki/Larry_Page "Larry Page") (right) in 2008

While conventional [search engines](/wiki/Search_engines "Search engines") ranked results by counting how many times the search terms appeared on the page, they theorized about a better system that analyzed the relationships among websites.[[30]](#cite_note-pagerank-31) They called this algorithm [PageRank](/wiki/PageRank "PageRank"); it determined a website's [relevance](/wiki/Relevance_(information_retrieval) "Relevance (information retrieval)") by the number of pages, and the importance of those pages that linked back to the original site.[[31]](#cite_note-32)[[30]](#cite_note-pagerank-31) Page told his ideas to Hassan, who began writing the code to implement Page's ideas.[[26]](#cite_note-vanityfair-27) Page and Brin would also use their friend [Susan Wojcicki](/wiki/Susan_Wojcicki "Susan Wojcicki")'s garage as their office when the search engine was set up in 1998.[[32]](#cite_note-33)

Page and Brin originally nicknamed the new search engine "BackRub" because the system checked backlinks to estimate the importance of a site.[[23]](#cite_note-howwestarted-24)[[33]](#cite_note-34)[[34]](#cite_note-35) Hassan, as well as Alan Steremberg were cited by Page and Brin as being critical to the development of Google. [Rajeev Motwani](/wiki/Rajeev_Motwani "Rajeev Motwani") and [Terry Winograd](/wiki/Terry_Winograd "Terry Winograd") later co-authored with Page and Brin the first paper about the project, describing PageRank and the initial prototype of the Google search engine, published in 1998. [Héctor García-Molina](/wiki/H%C3%A9ctor_Garc%C3%ADa-Molina "Héctor García-Molina") and [Jeffrey Ullman](/wiki/Jeffrey_Ullman "Jeffrey Ullman") were also cited as contributors to the project.[[35]](#cite_note-originalpaper-36) PageRank was influenced by a similar page-ranking and site-scoring algorithm earlier used for [RankDex](/wiki/Baidu#History "Baidu"), developed by [Robin Li](/wiki/Robin_Li "Robin Li") in 1996, with Page's PageRank patent including a citation to Li's earlier RankDex patent; Li later went on to create the Chinese search engine [Baidu](/wiki/Baidu "Baidu").[[36]](#cite_note-37)[[37]](#cite_note-38)

Eventually, they changed the name to *Google*; the name of the search engine was a misspelling of the word *[googol](/wiki/Googol "Googol")*,[[23]](#cite_note-howwestarted-24)[[38]](#cite_note-39)[[39]](#cite_note-Hanley,_Rachael-40) a very [large number](/wiki/Large_number "Large number") written **10100** (1 followed by 100 zeros), picked to signify that the search engine was intended to provide large quantities of information.[[40]](#cite_note-41) Google was initially funded by an August 1998 investment of $100,000 from [Andy Bechtolsheim](/wiki/Andy_Bechtolsheim "Andy Bechtolsheim"),[[23]](#cite_note-howwestarted-24) co-founder of [Sun Microsystems](/wiki/Sun_Microsystems "Sun Microsystems"). This initial investment served as a motivation to incorporate the company to be able to use the funds.[[41]](#cite_note-42)[[42]](#cite_note-Bechtolsheim-43) Page and Brin initially approached [David Cheriton](/wiki/David_Cheriton "David Cheriton") for advice because he had a nearby office in Stanford, and they knew he had startup experience, having recently sold the company he co-founded, Granite Systems, to [Cisco](/wiki/Cisco "Cisco") for $220 million. David arranged a meeting with Page and Brin and his Granite co-founder [Andy Bechtolsheim](/wiki/Andy_Bechtolsheim "Andy Bechtolsheim"). The meeting was set for 8 a.m. at the front porch of David's home in [Palo Alto](/wiki/Palo_Alto "Palo Alto") and it had to be brief because Andy had another meeting at [Cisco](/wiki/Cisco "Cisco"), where he now worked after the acquisition, at 9 a.m. Andy briefly tested a demo of the website, liked what he saw, and then went back to his car to grab the check. [David Cheriton](/wiki/David_Cheriton "David Cheriton") later also joined in with around $200,000 investment.[[43]](#cite_note-44)[[44]](#cite_note-45)

[![Google's homepage in 1998](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/b/b7/Google1998.png/330px-Google1998.png)](/wiki/File:Google1998.png)

Google's original homepage had a simplistic design because the company founders had little experience in [HTML](/wiki/HTML "HTML"), the [markup language](/wiki/Markup_language "Markup language") used for designing web pages.[[45]](#cite_note-46)

Google received money from two other [angel investors](/wiki/Angel_investor "Angel investor") in 1998, including [Amazon](/wiki/Amazon_(company) "Amazon (company)") founder [Jeff Bezos](/wiki/Jeff_Bezos "Jeff Bezos"), and entrepreneur [Ram Shriram](/wiki/Ram_Shriram "Ram Shriram").[[46]](#cite_note-endofworld-47) Page and Brin had first approached Shriram, who was a venture capitalist, for funding and counsel, and Shriram invested $250,000 in Google in February 1998. Shriram knew [Bezos](/wiki/Bezos "Bezos") because Amazon had acquired Junglee, at which Shriram was the president. It was Shriram who told Bezos about Google. Bezos asked Shriram to meet Google's founders and they met six months after Shriram had made his investment when Bezos and his wife were on a vacation trip to the Bay Area. Google's initial funding round had already formally closed but Bezos' status as CEO of Amazon was enough to persuade Page and Brin to extend the round and accept his investment.[[47]](#cite_note-48)[[48]](#cite_note-49) Between these initial investors, friends, and family, Google raised around $1,000,000, which is what allowed them to open up their original shop in [Menlo Park, California](/wiki/Menlo_Park,_California "Menlo Park, California").[[49]](#cite_note-Google_Inc-50) [Craig Silverstein](/wiki/Craig_Silverstein "Craig Silverstein"), a fellow PhD student at Stanford, was hired as the first employee.[[25]](#cite_note-milestones-26)[[50]](#cite_note-51)[[51]](#cite_note-52)

After some additional small investments through the end of 1998 to early 1999,[[46]](#cite_note-endofworld-47) a new $25 million round of funding was announced on June 7, 1999,[[52]](#cite_note-53) with major investors including the [venture capital](/wiki/Venture_capital "Venture capital") firms [Kleiner Perkins](/wiki/Kleiner_Perkins "Kleiner Perkins") and [Sequoia Capital](/wiki/Sequoia_Capital "Sequoia Capital").[[42]](#cite_note-Bechtolsheim-43) Both firms were initially hesitant about investing jointly in Google, as each wanted to retain a larger percentage of control over the company to themselves. Page and Brin insisted on taking investments from both. Both venture companies finally agreed to investing jointly $12.5 million each due to their belief in Google's great potential and through the mediation of earlier angel investors [Ron Conway](/wiki/Ron_Conway "Ron Conway") and Shriram who had contacts in the venture companies.[[53]](#cite_note-54) In 1998, Page and Brin proposed to sell Google to [Yahoo](/wiki/Yahoo "Yahoo") for $1 million, but Yahoo refused.[[54]](#cite_note-55) A more significant opportunity for Yahoo to acquire Google came in 2002. [Terry Semel](/wiki/Terry_Semel "Terry Semel"), Yahoo's then-CEO, offered $3 billion to purchase the company, but Page and Brin reportedly held firm on a $5 billion valuation. After Yahoo refused to raise its offer, the deal fell through, a move that would later be considered a major strategic misstep for Yahoo.[[55]](#cite_note-56)

### Growth

[![A picture of Google's first servers. The photo shows lots of primarily exposed beige wiring and multicolored circuit boards](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Google%E2%80%99s_First_Production_Server.jpg/250px-Google%E2%80%99s_First_Production_Server.jpg)](/wiki/File:Google%E2%80%99s_First_Production_Server.jpg)

Google's first production server[[56]](#cite_note-57)

In March 1999, the company moved its offices to [Palo Alto, California](/wiki/Palo_Alto,_California "Palo Alto, California"),[[57]](#cite_note-58) which is home to several prominent [Silicon Valley](/wiki/Silicon_Valley "Silicon Valley") technology start-ups.[[58]](#cite_note-59) The next year, Google began selling advertisements associated with search keywords against Page and Brin's initial opposition toward an advertising-funded search engine.[[59]](#cite_note-60)[[25]](#cite_note-milestones-26) To maintain an uncluttered page design, advertisements were solely text-based.[[60]](#cite_note-61) In June 2000, it was announced that Google would become the default search engine provider for [Yahoo!](/wiki/Yahoo! "Yahoo!"), one of the most popular websites at the time, replacing [Inktomi](/wiki/Inktomi "Inktomi").[[61]](#cite_note-62)[[62]](#cite_note-63)

In 2001, Google's investors felt the need to have a strong internal management, and they agreed to hire [Eric Schmidt](/wiki/Eric_Schmidt "Eric Schmidt") as the chairman and CEO of Google.[[49]](#cite_note-Google_Inc-50) Schmidt was proposed by [John Doerr](/wiki/John_Doerr "John Doerr") from Kleiner Perkins. He had been trying to find a CEO that Page and Brin would accept for several months, but they rejected several candidates because they wanted to retain control over the company. [Michael Moritz](/wiki/Michael_Moritz "Michael Moritz") from Sequoia Capital at one point even menaced requesting Google to immediately pay back Sequoia's $12.5m investment if they did not fulfill their promise to hire a chief executive officer, which had been made verbally during investment negotiations. Schmidt was not initially enthusiastic about joining Google either, as the company's full potential had not yet been widely recognized at the time, and as he was occupied with his responsibilities at [Novell](/wiki/Novell "Novell") where he was CEO. As part of him joining, Schmidt agreed to buy $1 million of Google preferred stocks as a way to show his commitment and to provide funds Google needed.[[63]](#cite_note-64)

In 2003, after outgrowing two other locations, the company leased an office complex from [Silicon Graphics](/wiki/Silicon_Graphics "Silicon Graphics"), at 1600 Amphitheatre Parkway in [Mountain View, California](/wiki/Mountain_View,_California "Mountain View, California").[[64]](#cite_note-sgibldg-65) The complex became known as the [Googleplex](/wiki/Googleplex "Googleplex"), a play on the word [googolplex](/wiki/Googolplex "Googolplex"), the number one followed by a googol of zeroes. Three years later, Google bought the property from SGI for $319 million.[[65]](#cite_note-googleplexpurchase-66) By that time, the name "Google" had found its way into everyday language, causing the verb "[google](/wiki/Google_(verb) "Google (verb)")" to be added to the *[Merriam-Webster Collegiate Dictionary](/wiki/Merriam-Webster_Collegiate_Dictionary "Merriam-Webster Collegiate Dictionary")* and the *[Oxford English Dictionary](/wiki/Oxford_English_Dictionary "Oxford English Dictionary")*, denoted as: "to use the Google search engine to obtain information on the Internet".[[66]](#cite_note-67)[[67]](#cite_note-google_or_not-68) The first use of the verb on television appeared in an October 2002 episode of *[Buffy the Vampire Slayer](/wiki/Buffy_the_Vampire_Slayer "Buffy the Vampire Slayer")*.[[68]](#cite_note-69)

### Initial public offering

[![Eric Schmidt](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Eric_Schmidt_at_the_37th_G8_Summit_in_Deauville_037.jpg/250px-Eric_Schmidt_at_the_37th_G8_Summit_in_Deauville_037.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eric_Schmidt_at_the_37th_G8_Summit_in_Deauville_037.jpg)

[Eric Schmidt](/wiki/Eric_Schmidt "Eric Schmidt"), CEO of Google from 2001 to 2011

On August 19, 2004, Google became a [public company](/wiki/Public_company "Public company") via an initial public offering. At that time Page, Brin and Schmidt agreed to work together at Google for 20 years, until the year 2024.[[69]](#cite_note-70) The company opened on the [NASDAQ National Market](/wiki/NASDAQ_National_Market "NASDAQ National Market") under the five-letter ticker symbol GOOGL with an offering of 19,605,052 shares at a price of $85 per share.[[70]](#cite_note-IPO-71)[[71]](#cite_note-GoogleAnnualReport2004-72) Shares were sold in an online auction format using a system built by [Morgan Stanley](/wiki/Morgan_Stanley "Morgan Stanley") and [Credit Suisse](/wiki/Credit_Suisse "Credit Suisse"), underwriters for the deal.[[72]](#cite_note-73)[[73]](#cite_note-74) The sale of $1.67 billion gave Google a [market capitalization](/wiki/Market_capitalization "Market capitalization") of more than $23 billion.[[74]](#cite_note-washpost-75)

On October 9, 2006, Google acquired [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube") for $1.65 billion in Google stock,[[75]](#cite_note-76)[[76]](#cite_note-77)[[77]](#cite_note-78)[[78]](#cite_note-79) On July 20, 2007, Google bids $4.6 billion for the wireless-spectrum auction by the [FCC](/wiki/FCC "FCC").[[79]](#cite_note-80) On March 11, 2008, Google acquired [DoubleClick](/wiki/DoubleClick "DoubleClick") for $3.1 billion, transferring to Google valuable relationships that DoubleClick had with Web publishers and advertising agencies.[[80]](#cite_note-81)[[81]](#cite_note-82) By 2011, Google was handling approximately 3 billion searches per day. To handle this workload, Google built 11 [data centers](/wiki/Data_centers "Data centers") around the world with several thousand servers in each. These data centers allowed Google to handle the ever-changing workload more efficiently.[[49]](#cite_note-Google_Inc-50)

In May 2011, the number of monthly unique visitors to Google surpassed one billion for the first time.[[82]](#cite_note-83)[[83]](#cite_note-84) In May 2012, Google acquired [Motorola Mobility](/wiki/Motorola_Mobility "Motorola Mobility") for $12.5 billion, in its largest acquisition to date.[[84]](#cite_note-85)[[85]](#cite_note-86)[[86]](#cite_note-87) This purchase was made in part to help Google gain Motorola's considerable patent portfolio on mobile phones and wireless technologies, to help protect Google in its ongoing patent disputes with other companies,[[87]](#cite_note-88) mainly [Apple](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc.") and [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft"),[[88]](#cite_note-89) and to allow it to continue to freely offer Android.[[89]](#cite_note-cnet-90)

### 2012 onwards

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Google-Deep_Mind_headquarters_in_London%2C_6_Pancras_Square.jpg/250px-Google-Deep_Mind_headquarters_in_London%2C_6_Pancras_Square.jpg)](/wiki/File:Google-Deep_Mind_headquarters_in_London,_6_Pancras_Square.jpg)

Entrance of building where Google and its subsidiary DeepMind are located at 6 Pancras Square, London

In June 2013, Google acquired [Waze](/wiki/Waze "Waze") for $966 million.[[90]](#cite_note-91) While Waze would remain an independent entity, its social features, such as its crowdsourced location platform, were reportedly valuable integrations between Waze and [Google Maps](/wiki/Google_Maps "Google Maps"), Google's own mapping service.[[91]](#cite_note-92) Google announced the launch of a new company, called [Calico](/wiki/Calico_(company) "Calico (company)"), on September 19, 2013, to be led by Apple Inc. chairman [Arthur Levinson](/wiki/Arthur_D._Levinson "Arthur D. Levinson"). In the official public statement, Page explained that the "health and well-being" company would focus on "the challenge of ageing and associated diseases".[[92]](#cite_note-93)

On January 26, 2014, Google announced it had agreed to acquire [DeepMind Technologies](/wiki/DeepMind_Technologies "DeepMind Technologies"), a privately held AI company from [London](/wiki/London "London").[[93]](#cite_note-94) Technology news website *[Recode](/wiki/Recode "Recode")* reported that the company was purchased for $400 million, yet the source of the information was not disclosed. A Google spokesperson declined to comment on the price.[[94]](#cite_note-Helgren-_DeepMind-95)[[95]](#cite_note-Ribeiro-_DeepMind-96) The purchase of DeepMind aids in Google's recent growth in the AI and robotics community.[[96]](#cite_note-97) In 2015, DeepMind's [AlphaGo](/wiki/AlphaGo "AlphaGo") became the first computer program to [defeat a top human pro](/wiki/AlphaGo_versus_Lee_Sedol "AlphaGo versus Lee Sedol") at the game of Go. According to Interbrand's annual Best Global Brands report, Google has been the second most valuable brand in the world (behind Apple Inc.) in 2013,[[97]](#cite_note-98) 2014,[[98]](#cite_note-99) 2015,[[99]](#cite_note-100) and 2016, with a valuation of $133 billion.[[100]](#cite_note-101)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/The_CEO_of_Google%2C_Mr._Sundar_Pichai_calls_on_the_Prime_Minister%2C_Shri_Narendra_Modi%2C_in_New_Delhi_on_December_17%2C_2015_%281%29.jpg/250px-The_CEO_of_Google%2C_Mr._Sundar_Pichai_calls_on_the_Prime_Minister%2C_Shri_Narendra_Modi%2C_in_New_Delhi_on_December_17%2C_2015_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_CEO_of_Google,_Mr._Sundar_Pichai_calls_on_the_Prime_Minister,_Shri_Narendra_Modi,_in_New_Delhi_on_December_17,_2015_(1).jpg)

Google CEO [Sundar Pichai](/wiki/Sundar_Pichai "Sundar Pichai") with Indian prime minister [Narendra Modi](/wiki/Narendra_Modi "Narendra Modi")

On August 10, 2015, Google announced plans to reorganize its various interests as a [conglomerate](/wiki/Conglomerate_(company) "Conglomerate (company)") named Alphabet Inc. Google became Alphabet's largest subsidiary and the [umbrella company](/wiki/Umbrella_company "Umbrella company") for Alphabet's Internet interests. Upon completion of the restructuring, Sundar Pichai became [CEO](/wiki/CEO "CEO") of Google, replacing Page, who became CEO of Alphabet.[[101]](#cite_note-102)[[102]](#cite_note-103)[[103]](#cite_note-104) On August 8, 2017, Google fired employee [James Damore](/wiki/James_Damore "James Damore") after he distributed a memo throughout the company that argued bias and "[Google's Ideological Echo Chamber](/wiki/Google%27s_Ideological_Echo_Chamber "Google's Ideological Echo Chamber")" clouded their thinking about diversity and inclusion, and that it is also biological factors, not discrimination alone, that cause the average woman to be less interested than men in technical positions.[[104]](#cite_note-105) Google CEO Sundar Pichai accused Damore of violating company policy by "advancing harmful gender stereotypes in our workplace", and he was fired on the same day.[[105]](#cite_note-106)[[106]](#cite_note-107)[[107]](#cite_note-108)

Between 2018 and 2019, [tensions between the company's leadership and its workers escalated](/wiki/Google_worker_organization "Google worker organization") as staff protested company decisions on internal sexual harassment, [Dragonfly](/wiki/Dragonfly_(search_engine) "Dragonfly (search engine)"), a censored Chinese search engine, and [Project Maven](/wiki/Project_Maven "Project Maven"), a military drone artificial intelligence, which had been seen as areas of revenue growth for the company.[[108]](#cite_note-109)[[109]](#cite_note-Verge_busting-110) On October 25, 2018, *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* published the *exposé*, "How Google Protected [Andy Rubin](/wiki/Andy_Rubin "Andy Rubin"), the 'Father of Android'". The company subsequently announced that "48 employees have been fired over the last two years" for sexual misconduct.[[110]](#cite_note-111) On November 1, 2018, more than 20,000 Google employees and contractors staged a global walk-out to protest the company's handling of sexual harassment complaints.[[111]](#cite_note-112)[[112]](#cite_note-113) CEO Sundar Pichai was reported to be in support of the protests.[[113]](#cite_note-114) Later in 2019, some workers accused the company of retaliating against internal activists.[[109]](#cite_note-Verge_busting-110)

On March 19, 2019, Google announced that it would enter the video game market, launching a [cloud gaming](/wiki/Cloud_gaming "Cloud gaming") platform called [Google Stadia](/wiki/Google_Stadia "Google Stadia").[[114]](#cite_note-unveils-115) On June 3, 2019, the [U.S. Department of Justice](/wiki/U.S._Department_of_Justice "U.S. Department of Justice") reported that it would investigate Google for [antitrust](/wiki/Antitrust "Antitrust") violations.[[115]](#cite_note-116) This led to the filing of an antitrust lawsuit in October 2020, on the grounds the company had abused a monopoly position in the [search](/wiki/Web_search_engine "Web search engine") and [search advertising](/wiki/Search_advertising "Search advertising") markets.[[116]](#cite_note-117) In December 2019, former [PayPal](/wiki/PayPal "PayPal") [chief operating officer](/wiki/Chief_operating_officer "Chief operating officer") [Bill Ready](/wiki/Bill_Ready "Bill Ready") became Google's new commerce chief. Ready's role will not be directly involved with [Google Pay](/wiki/Google_Pay_(2018%E2%80%932022) "Google Pay (2018–2022)").[[117]](#cite_note-118) In April 2020, due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), Google announced several cost-cutting measures. Such measures included slowing down hiring for the remainder of 2020, except for a small number of strategic areas, recalibrating the focus and pace of investments in areas like data centers and machines, and non-business essential marketing and travel.[[118]](#cite_note-119) Most employees were also working from home due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic") and the success of it even led to Google announcing that they would be permanently converting some of their jobs to work from home [[119]](#cite_note-120)

The [2020 Google services outages](/wiki/2020_Google_services_outages "2020 Google services outages") disrupted Google services: one in August that affected [Google Drive](/wiki/Google_Drive "Google Drive") among others, another in November affecting [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube"), and a third in December affecting the entire suite of Google applications. All three outages were resolved within hours.[[120]](#cite_note-121)[[121]](#cite_note-122)[[122]](#cite_note-123) In 2021, the [Alphabet Workers Union](/wiki/Alphabet_Workers_Union "Alphabet Workers Union") was founded, composed mostly of Google employees.[[123]](#cite_note-124) In January 2021, the [Australian Government](/wiki/Australian_Government "Australian Government") proposed legislation that would require Google and Facebook to pay media companies for the right to use their content. In response, Google threatened to close off access to its search engine in Australia.[[124]](#cite_note-125) In March 2021, Google reportedly paid $20 million for [Ubisoft](/wiki/Ubisoft "Ubisoft") ports on Google Stadia.[[125]](#cite_note-126) Google spent "tens of millions of dollars" on getting major publishers such as [Ubisoft](/wiki/Ubisoft "Ubisoft") and [Take-Two](/wiki/Take-Two "Take-Two") to bring some of their biggest games to Stadia.[[126]](#cite_note-127) In April 2021, *[The Wall Street Journal](/wiki/The_Wall_Street_Journal "The Wall Street Journal")* reported that Google ran a years-long program called "Project Bernanke" that used data from past advertising bids to gain an advantage over competing for ad services. This was revealed in documents concerning the antitrust lawsuit filed by ten US states against Google in December.[[127]](#cite_note-128) In September 2021, the Australian government announced plans to curb Google's capability to sell targeted ads, claiming that the company has a monopoly on the market harming publishers, advertisers, and consumers.[[128]](#cite_note-129)

In 2022, Google began accepting requests for the removal of phone numbers, physical addresses and email addresses from its search results. It had previously accepted requests for removing confidential data only, such as Social Security numbers, bank account and credit card numbers, personal signatures, and medical records. Even with the new policy, Google may remove information from only certain but not all search queries. It would not remove content that is "broadly useful", such as news articles, or already part of the public record.[[129]](#cite_note-130) In May 2022, Google announced that the company had acquired California based, MicroLED display technology development and manufacturing Start-up company Raxium. Raxium is set to join Google's Devices and Services team to aid in the development of micro-optics, monolithic integration, and system integration.[[130]](#cite_note-d065-131)[[131]](#cite_note-132) In December 2022, Google debuted OSV-Scanner,[[132]](#cite_note-133)[[133]](#cite_note-134) a [Go](/wiki/Go_(programming_language) "Go (programming language)") tool for finding [security holes](/wiki/Security_hole "Security hole") in [open source software](/wiki/Open_source_software "Open source software"), which pulls from the largest open source [vulnerability database](/wiki/Vulnerability_database "Vulnerability database") of its kind to defend against [supply chain](/wiki/Software_supply_chain "Software supply chain") attacks. Following the success of [ChatGPT](/wiki/ChatGPT "ChatGPT") and concerns that Google was falling behind in the AI race, Google's senior management issued a "code red"[[134]](#cite_note-135) and a "directive that all of its most important products—those with more than a billion users—must incorporate generative AI within months".[[135]](#cite_note-136) In March 2023, in direct response to the rapid rise of ChatGPT, Google released Bard (now [Gemini](/wiki/Gemini_(chatbot) "Gemini (chatbot)")), a [generative artificial intelligence](/wiki/Generative_artificial_intelligence "Generative artificial intelligence") [chatbot](/wiki/Chatbot "Chatbot").[[136]](#cite_note-137)

In early May 2023, Google announced its plans to build two additional data centers in Ohio. These centers, which will be built in Columbus and Lancaster, will power up the company's tools, including AI technology. The said data hub will add to the already operational center near Columbus, bringing Google's total investment in Ohio to over $2 billion.[[137]](#cite_note-138) In August 2024, Google would lose a [lawsuit which started in 2020](/wiki/United_States_v._Google_LLC_(2020) "United States v. Google LLC (2020)") in lower court, as it was found that the company had an illegal monopoly over Internet search.[[138]](#cite_note-139) D.C. Circuit Court Judge Amit Mehta held that this monopoly was in violation of Section 2 of the [Sherman Act](/wiki/Sherman_Act "Sherman Act").[[139]](#cite_note-140) In September 2024, the [Court of Justice of the European Union](/wiki/Court_of_Justice_of_the_European_Union "Court of Justice of the European Union") (EU), based in Luxembourg, also found that Google held an illegal monopoly, in this case with regards to its shopping search, and could not avoid paying a €2.4 billion fine.[[140]](#cite_note-eushoppingruling-141) The EU Court of Justice found that Google's treatment of rival shopping searches, which the court referred to as "discriminatory", was in violation of the [Digital Markets Act](/wiki/Digital_Markets_Act "Digital Markets Act").[[140]](#cite_note-eushoppingruling-141) In October 2024, Google was fined by a local Russian court a symbolic 2.5 decillion dollars for allegedly blocking pro-Kremlin propaganda. No payment was made.[[141]](#cite_note-142)

In November 2024, Google announced the establishment of a new AI hub in Saudi Arabia, aiming to support the Kingdom's economic growth and technological development as part of its Vision 2030 initiative. This AI hub is projected to contribute up to $71 billion to Saudi Arabia's economy by advancing AI-driven solutions tailored to the region's specific needs and training local talent.[[142]](#cite_note-143) The partnership between Google and Saudi Arabia includes collaboration with key stakeholders, such as the Public Investment Fund (PIF), to develop AI applications that will benefit sectors like healthcare, finance, oil and gas, and logistics. The initiative focuses on creating localized AI technologies, with an emphasis on integrating Arabic language capabilities and enabling widespread cloud adoption.[[143]](#cite_note-144)

In March 2025, Google agreed to acquire [Wiz](/wiki/Wiz,_Inc. "Wiz, Inc."), a New York-based [cybersecurity](/wiki/Cybersecurity "Cybersecurity") startup focusing on cloud computing, for US$32 billion. This cash deal would be Google's biggest ever, as well as it currently being the most expensive deal of 2025. Alphabet reportedly tried to close a deal for only $23 billion in 2024, but this fell apart after concerns about regulatory hurdles, among other issues. Wiz, a company located in the U.S. and Israel, was cofounded in 2020 by [Assaf Rappaport](/wiki/Assaf_Rappaport "Assaf Rappaport"). The company is backed by a number of Silicon Valley venture capitalists, as well as notably being partnered with Amazon and Microsoft, as listed in their website. Google reportedly said "the deal would help artificial-intelligence companies get better security and use more than one cloud service."[[144]](#cite_note-145)

In July 2025, the U.S. Department of Defense announced that Google had received a $200 million [contract](/wiki/Contract "Contract") for AI in the military, along with [Anthropic](/wiki/Anthropic "Anthropic"), [OpenAI](/wiki/OpenAI "OpenAI"), and [xAI](/wiki/XAI_(company) "XAI (company)").[[145]](#cite_note-146) In September 2025, federal judge Amit Mehta in the United States ruled that Google will not be required to divest Chrome or the Android operating system;[[146]](#cite_note-147) however, the ruling barred Google from having exclusive contracts for Google Search, Chrome, Google Assistant and Gemini app products, and ruled Google must share search data with competitors.[[147]](#cite_note-148)

In March 2026, Google signed an energy pledge at the White House which required them to bear the cost of new electricity generation to power their data centers.[[148]](#cite_note-149)

## Products and services

Main article: [List of Google products](/wiki/List_of_Google_products "List of Google products")

### Search engine

Main articles: [Google Search](/wiki/Google_Search "Google Search") and [Google Images](/wiki/Google_Images "Google Images")

Google [indexes](/wiki/Search_engine_indexing "Search engine indexing") billions of web pages to allow users to search for the information they desire through the use of keywords and [operators](/wiki/Operator_(computer_programming) "Operator (computer programming)").[[149]](#cite_note-150) According to [comScore](/wiki/ComScore "ComScore") market research from November 2009, Google Search is the dominant search engine in the United States market, with a [market share](/wiki/Market_share "Market share") of 65.6%.[[150]](#cite_note-comscore-151) In May 2017, Google enabled a new "Personal" tab in Google Search, letting users search for content in their Google accounts' various services, including email messages from Gmail and photos from Google Photos.[[151]](#cite_note-152)[[152]](#cite_note-153)

Google launched its [Google News](/wiki/Google_News "Google News") service in 2002, an automated service which summarizes news articles from various websites.[[153]](#cite_note-154) Google also hosts [Google Books](/wiki/Google_Books "Google Books"), which allows users to search books in its database and shows limited previews, or the full book when allowed.[[154]](#cite_note-155) Google expanded its search services to include [shopping](/wiki/Google_Shopping "Google Shopping") (launched originally as Froogle in 2002),[[155]](#cite_note-156) [finance](/wiki/Google_Finance "Google Finance") (launched 2006),[[156]](#cite_note-157) and [flights](/wiki/Google_Flights "Google Flights") (launched 2011).[[157]](#cite_note-158)

### Advertising

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Ad-tech_London_2010_%282%29.JPG/250px-Ad-tech_London_2010_%282%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Ad-tech_London_2010_(2).JPG)

Google at ad-tech London, 2010

Google generates most of its revenues from advertising. This includes sales of apps, purchases made in-app, digital content products on Google and YouTube, Android and licensing and service fees, including fees received for Google Cloud offerings. Forty-six percent of this profit was from clicks (cost per clicks), amounting to US$109,652 million in 2017. This includes three principal methods, namely [AdMob](/wiki/AdMob "AdMob"), [AdSense](/wiki/AdSense "AdSense") (such as AdSense for Content, AdSense for Search, etc.) and [DoubleClick](/wiki/DoubleClick "DoubleClick") AdExchange.[[158]](#cite_note-agm2017-159) In addition to its own algorithms for understanding search requests, Google uses technology from its acquisition of [DoubleClick](/wiki/DoubleClick "DoubleClick"), to project user interest and target advertising to the search context and the user history.[[159]](#cite_note-160)[[160]](#cite_note-161) In 2007, Google launched "[AdSense for Mobile](/wiki/AdSense_for_Mobile "AdSense for Mobile")", taking advantage of the emerging mobile advertising market.[[161]](#cite_note-adsense_mobile-162)

[Google Analytics](/wiki/Google_Analytics "Google Analytics") allows website owners to track where and how people use their website, for example by examining click rates for all the links on a page.[[162]](#cite_note-163) Google advertisements can be placed on third-party websites in a two-part program. [Google Ads](/wiki/Google_Ads "Google Ads") allows advertisers to display their advertisements in the Google content network, through a cost-per-click scheme.[[163]](#cite_note-164) The sister service, Google AdSense, allows website owners to display these advertisements on their website and earn money every time ads are clicked.[[164]](#cite_note-165) One of the criticisms of this program is the possibility of [click fraud](/wiki/Click_fraud "Click fraud"), which occurs when a person or automated script clicks on advertisements without being interested in the product, causing the advertiser to pay money to Google unduly. Industry reports in 2006 claimed that approximately 14 to 20 percent of clicks were fraudulent or invalid.[[165]](#cite_note-166) [Google Search Console](/wiki/Google_Search_Console "Google Search Console") (rebranded from Google Webmaster Tools in May 2015) allows [webmasters](/wiki/Webmasters "Webmasters") to check the sitemap, crawl rate, and for security issues of their websites, as well as optimize their website's visibility.

### Generative artificial intelligence

Google had previously used [virtual assistants](/wiki/Virtual_assistant "Virtual assistant") and [chatbots](/wiki/Chatbot "Chatbot"), such as Google Bard, prior to the announcement of [Gemini](/wiki/Gemini_(chatbot) "Gemini (chatbot)") in March 2024. None of them had been seen as legitimate competitors to [ChatGPT](/wiki/ChatGPT "ChatGPT"), unlike Gemini.[[166]](#cite_note-167) An AI training program for Google employees was also introduced in April 2024.[[167]](#cite_note-168) Google has created the [text-to-image model](/wiki/Text-to-image_model "Text-to-image model") [Imagen](/wiki/Imagen_(text-to-image_model) "Imagen (text-to-image model)"),[[168]](#cite_note-169) and the [text-to-video model](/wiki/Text-to-video_model "Text-to-video model") [Veo](/wiki/Veo_(text-to-video_model) "Veo (text-to-video model)").[[169]](#cite_note-170) Google integrated [AI Overviews](/wiki/AI_Overviews "AI Overviews") into Google Search, and added an AI mode where the search results page is AI-generated.[[170]](#cite_note-171) In 2025, Google announced SynthID Detector, a tool that uses [watermarking](/wiki/Digital_watermarking "Digital watermarking") to identify whether content such as text, images, audio, or video was generated using Google products.[[171]](#cite_note-172) In 2023, Google released [NotebookLM](/wiki/NotebookLM "NotebookLM"), an online tool for synthesizing documents using Gemini. In September 2024, it gained attention for its "Audio Overview" feature, which generates podcast-like summaries of documents.[[172]](#cite_note-173)[[173]](#cite_note-174) Google also developed LearnLM, a family of language models serving as personal [AI tutors](/wiki/AI_tutor "AI tutor").[[174]](#cite_note-175)

### Consumer services

#### Web-based services

See also: [Firebase Studio](/wiki/Firebase_Studio "Firebase Studio"), [Google Colab](/wiki/Google_Colab "Google Colab"), [Kaggle](/wiki/Kaggle "Kaggle"), and [Google Docs Editors](/wiki/Google_Docs_Editors "Google Docs Editors")

Google offers [Gmail](/wiki/Gmail "Gmail") for [email](/wiki/Email "Email"),[[175]](#cite_note-176) [Google Calendar](/wiki/Google_Calendar "Google Calendar") for time-management and scheduling,[[176]](#cite_note-177) [Google Maps](/wiki/Google_Maps "Google Maps") and Google Earth for mapping, navigation and [satellite imagery](/wiki/Satellite_imagery "Satellite imagery"),[[177]](#cite_note-178) Google Drive for [cloud storage](/wiki/File_hosting_service "File hosting service") of files,[[178]](#cite_note-verge-drive-announced-179) [Google Docs](/wiki/Google_Docs "Google Docs"), [Sheets](/wiki/Google_Sheets "Google Sheets") and [Slides](/wiki/Google_Slides "Google Slides") for productivity,[[178]](#cite_note-verge-drive-announced-179) [Google Photos](/wiki/Google_Photos "Google Photos") for photo storage and sharing,[[179]](#cite_note-180) [Google Keep](/wiki/Google_Keep "Google Keep") for [note-taking](/wiki/Note-taking "Note-taking"),[[180]](#cite_note-181) [Google Translate](/wiki/Google_Translate "Google Translate") for language translation,[[181]](#cite_note-182) [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube") for video viewing and sharing,[[182]](#cite_note-183) [Google My Business](/wiki/Google_My_Business "Google My Business") for managing public business information,[[183]](#cite_note-184) [Google Classroom](/wiki/Google_Classroom "Google Classroom") for managing assignments and communication in education,[[184]](#cite_note-185) and [Duo](/wiki/Google_Duo "Google Duo") for social interaction.[[185]](#cite_note-186) A job search product has also existed since before 2017,[[186]](#cite_note-187)[[187]](#cite_note-188)[[188]](#cite_note-189) Google for Jobs is an enhanced search feature that aggregates listings from [job boards](/wiki/Job_board "Job board") and career sites.[[189]](#cite_note-190) [Google Earth](/wiki/Google_Earth "Google Earth"), launched in 2005, allows users to see high-definition satellite pictures from all over the world for free through a client software downloaded to their computers.[[190]](#cite_note-191)

#### Software

Google develops the Android [mobile operating system](/wiki/Mobile_operating_system "Mobile operating system"),[[191]](#cite_note-192) as well as its [smartwatch](/wiki/Wear_OS "Wear OS"),[[192]](#cite_note-193) [television](/wiki/Android_TV "Android TV"),[[193]](#cite_note-194) [car](/wiki/Android_Auto "Android Auto"),[[194]](#cite_note-195) and [Internet of things](/wiki/Internet_of_things "Internet of things")-enabled [smart devices](/wiki/Android_Things "Android Things") variations.[[195]](#cite_note-196) It also develops the [Google Chrome](/wiki/Google_Chrome "Google Chrome") web browser,[[196]](#cite_note-197) [ChromeOS](/wiki/ChromeOS "ChromeOS"), an operating system based on Chrome,[[197]](#cite_note-198) and the AI-powered [integrated development environment](/wiki/Integrated_development_environment "Integrated development environment") [Google Antigravity](/wiki/Google_Antigravity "Google Antigravity").[[198]](#cite_note-199)[[199]](#cite_note-200)

#### Hardware

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Pixel_3_%E3%81%A8_Pixel_3_XL_%E3%82%92%E5%88%9D%E8%A7%A6%E3%80%82%E6%9C%AC%E4%BD%93%E3%82%92%E3%82%AE%E3%83%A5%E3%83%83%E3%81%A8%E6%8F%A1%E3%82%8B%E3%81%A8_Google_Assistant_%E3%81%8C%E7%AB%8B%E3%81%A1%E4%B8%8A%E3%81%8C%E3%82%8B%E3%81%AE%E3%81%8C%E3%81%8A%E3%82%82%E3%81%97%E3%82%8D%E3%81%84%E3%80%82_%E3%83%AF%E3%82%B7%E3%83%B3%E3%83%88%E3%83%B3DC_%2844519013945%29.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pixel_3_%E3%81%A8_Pixel_3_XL_%E3%82%92%E5%88%9D%E8%A7%A6%E3%80%82%E6%9C%AC%E4%BD%93%E3%82%92%E3%82%AE%E3%83%A5%E3%83%83%E3%81%A8%E6%8F%A1%E3%82%8B%E3%81%A8_Google_Assistant_%E3%81%8C%E7%AB%8B%E3%81%A1%E4%B8%8A%E3%81%8C%E3%82%8B%E3%81%AE%E3%81%8C%E3%81%8A%E3%82%82%E3%81%97%E3%82%8D%E3%81%84%E3%80%82_%E3%83%AF%E3%82%B7%E3%83%B3%E3%83%88%E3%83%B3DC_(44519013945).jpg)

Google Pixel smartphones on display in a store

In January 2010, Google released [Nexus One](/wiki/Nexus_One "Nexus One"), the first Android phone under its own brand.[[200]](#cite_note-201) It spawned a number of phones and tablets under the "[Nexus](/wiki/Google_Nexus "Google Nexus")" branding[[201]](#cite_note-202) until its eventual discontinuation in 2016, replaced by a new brand called [Pixel](/wiki/Google_Pixel "Google Pixel").[[202]](#cite_note-Pixel_inside_story-203) In 2011, the [Chromebook](/wiki/Chromebook "Chromebook") was introduced, which runs on [ChromeOS](/wiki/ChromeOS "ChromeOS").[[203]](#cite_note-204) In July 2013, Google introduced the [Chromecast](/wiki/Chromecast "Chromecast") dongle, which allows users to stream content from their smartphones to televisions.[[204]](#cite_note-205)[[205]](#cite_note-206) In June 2014, Google announced [Google Cardboard](/wiki/Google_Cardboard "Google Cardboard"), a simple cardboard viewer that lets the user place their smartphone in a special front compartment to view [virtual reality](/wiki/Virtual_reality "Virtual reality") (VR) media.[[206]](#cite_note-207) In October 2016, Google announced [Daydream View](/wiki/Daydream_View "Daydream View"), a lightweight VR viewer which lets the user place their smartphone in the front hinge to view VR media.[[207]](#cite_note-208)[[208]](#cite_note-209)

Other hardware products include:

* [Nest](/wiki/Google_Nest_(smart_speakers) "Google Nest (smart speakers)"), a series of voice assistant smart speakers that can answer voice queries, play music, find information from apps (calendar, weather etc.), and control third-party smart home appliances (users can tell it to turn on the lights, for example). The Google Nest line includes the original [Google Home](/wiki/Google_Home_(smart_speaker) "Google Home (smart speaker)")[[209]](#cite_note-210) (later succeeded by the [Nest Audio](/wiki/Nest_Audio "Nest Audio")), the [Google Home Mini](/wiki/Google_Home_Mini "Google Home Mini") (later succeeded by the [Nest Mini](/wiki/Nest_Mini "Nest Mini")), the [Google Home Max](/wiki/Google_Home_Max "Google Home Max"), the [Google Home Hub](/wiki/Google_Home_Hub "Google Home Hub") (later rebranded as the Nest Hub), and the [Nest Hub Max](/wiki/Nest_Hub_Max "Nest Hub Max").
* [Nest Wifi](/wiki/Google_Nest_Wifi "Google Nest Wifi") (originally Google Wifi), a connected set of [Wi-Fi](/wiki/Wi-Fi "Wi-Fi") routers to simplify and extend coverage of home Wi-Fi.[[210]](#cite_note-211)

### Enterprise services

Main articles: [Google Workspace](/wiki/Google_Workspace "Google Workspace") and [Google Cloud Platform](/wiki/Google_Cloud_Platform "Google Cloud Platform")

Google Workspace (formerly G Suite until October 2020[[211]](#cite_note-212)) is a monthly subscription offering for organizations and businesses to get access to a collection of Google's services, including Gmail, Google Drive and [Google Docs](/wiki/Google_Docs "Google Docs"), [Google Sheets](/wiki/Google_Sheets "Google Sheets") and [Google Slides](/wiki/Google_Slides "Google Slides"), with additional administrative tools, unique domain names, and 24/7 support.[[212]](#cite_note-213) On September 24, 2012,[[213]](#cite_note-214) Google launched [Google for Entrepreneurs](/wiki/Google_for_Entrepreneurs "Google for Entrepreneurs"), a largely not-for-profit [business incubator](/wiki/Business_incubator "Business incubator") providing startups with [co-working spaces](/wiki/Coworking "Coworking") known as Campuses, with assistance to startup founders that may include workshops, conferences, and mentorships.[[214]](#cite_note-215) There are seven Campus locations: [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin"), [London](/wiki/London "London"), [Madrid](/wiki/Madrid "Madrid"), [Seoul](/wiki/Seoul "Seoul"), [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Paulo"), [Tel Aviv](/wiki/Tel_Aviv "Tel Aviv"), and [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw"). On March 15, 2016, Google announced the introduction of Google Analytics 360 Suite, "a set of integrated data and marketing analytics products, designed specifically for the needs of enterprise-class marketers" which can be integrated with [BigQuery](/wiki/BigQuery "BigQuery") on the Google Cloud Platform. Among other things, the suite is designed to help "enterprise class marketers" "see the complete [customer journey](/wiki/Customer_journey "Customer journey")", generate "useful insights", and "deliver engaging experiences to the right people".[[215]](#cite_note-216) Jack Marshall of *[The Wall Street Journal](/wiki/The_Wall_Street_Journal "The Wall Street Journal")* wrote that the suite competes with existing marketing cloud offerings by companies including [Adobe](/wiki/Adobe_Systems "Adobe Systems"), [Oracle](/wiki/Oracle_Corporation "Oracle Corporation"), [Salesforce](/wiki/Salesforce.com "Salesforce.com"), and [IBM](/wiki/IBM "IBM").[[216]](#cite_note-217)

### Internet services

In February 2010, Google announced the [Google Fiber](/wiki/Google_Fiber "Google Fiber") project, with experimental plans to build an ultra-high-speed broadband network for 50,000 to 500,000 customers in one or more American cities.[[217]](#cite_note-218)[[218]](#cite_note-219) Following Google's corporate restructure to make Alphabet Inc. its parent company, [Google Fiber](/wiki/Google_Fiber "Google Fiber") was moved to Alphabet's Access division.[[219]](#cite_note-220)[[220]](#cite_note-221) In April 2015, Google announced [Project Fi](/wiki/Project_Fi "Project Fi"), a mobile virtual network operator, that combines Wi-Fi and cellular networks from different telecommunication providers in an effort to enable seamless connectivity and fast Internet signal.[[221]](#cite_note-222)[[222]](#cite_note-223)

### Financial services

In August 2023, Google became the first major tech company to join the [OpenWallet Foundation](/wiki/OpenWallet_Foundation "OpenWallet Foundation"), launched earlier in the year, whose goal was creating open-source software for interoperable digital wallets.[[223]](#cite_note-224)
