Google's [initial public offering](/wiki/Initial_public_offering "Initial public offering") (IPO) took place on August 19, 2004. At IPO, the company offered 19,605,052 shares at a price of $85 per share.[[70]](#cite_note-IPO-71)[[71]](#cite_note-GoogleAnnualReport2004-72) The sale of $1.67 billion gave Google a [market capitalization](/wiki/Market_capitalization "Market capitalization") of more than $23 billion.[[74]](#cite_note-washpost-75) The stock performed well after the IPO, with shares hitting $350 for the first time on October 31, 2007,[[230]](#cite_note-231) primarily because of strong sales and earnings in the [online advertising](/wiki/Online_advertising "Online advertising") market.[[231]](#cite_note-bowlingforgoogle-232) The surge in stock price was fueled mainly by individual investors, as opposed to large institutional investors and [mutual funds](/wiki/Mutual_fund "Mutual fund").[[231]](#cite_note-bowlingforgoogle-232) GOOG shares split into GOOG [class C shares](/wiki/Class_C_share "Class C share") and GOOGL [class A shares](/wiki/Class_A_share "Class A share").[[232]](#cite_note-233) The company is listed on the [NASDAQ](/wiki/NASDAQ "NASDAQ") stock exchange under the [ticker symbols](/wiki/Ticker_symbol "Ticker symbol") GOOGL and GOOG, and on the [Frankfurt Stock Exchange](/wiki/Frankfurt_Stock_Exchange "Frankfurt Stock Exchange") under the ticker symbol GGQ1. These ticker symbols now refer to Alphabet Inc., Google's holding company, since the fourth quarter of 2015.[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Google&action=edit)[[233]](#cite_note-234)

In the third quarter of 2005, Google reported a 700% increase in profit, largely due to large companies shifting their advertising strategies from newspapers, magazines, and television to the Internet.[[234]](#cite_note-235)[[235]](#cite_note-236)[[236]](#cite_note-237) For the 2006 fiscal year, the company reported $10.492 billion in total advertising revenues and only $112 million in licensing and other revenues.[[237]](#cite_note-10-K-238) In 2011, 96% of Google's revenue was derived from its advertising programs.[[238]](#cite_note-Google-Inc-Jan-2012-10-K-239) Google generated $50 billion in annual revenue for the first time in 2012, generating $38 billion the previous year. In January 2013, then-CEO Larry Page commented, "We ended 2012 with a strong quarter ... Revenues were up 36% year-on-year, and 8% quarter-on-quarter. And we hit $50 billion in revenues for the first time last year – not a bad achievement in just a decade and a half."[[239]](#cite_note-240) Google's consolidated revenue for the third quarter of 2013 was reported in mid-October 2013 as $14.89 billion, a 12 percent increase compared to the previous quarter.[[240]](#cite_note-241) Google's Internet business was responsible for $10.8 billion of this total, with an increase in the number of users' clicks on advertisements.[[241]](#cite_note-242) By January 2014, Google's market capitalization had grown to $397 billion.[[242]](#cite_note-Marketwatch-243)

### Tax avoidance strategies

Further information: [Corporation tax in the Republic of Ireland § Multinational tax schemes](/wiki/Corporation_tax_in_the_Republic_of_Ireland#Multinational_tax_schemes "Corporation tax in the Republic of Ireland"), and [Google tax](/wiki/Google_tax "Google tax")

Google uses various [tax avoidance](/wiki/Tax_avoidance "Tax avoidance") strategies. On the [list of largest technology companies by revenue](/wiki/List_of_largest_technology_companies_by_revenue "List of largest technology companies by revenue"), it pays the lowest taxes to the countries of origin of its revenues. Google between 2007 and 2010 saved $3.1 billion in taxes by shuttling non-U.S. profits through [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland") and the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") and then to [Bermuda](/wiki/Bermuda "Bermuda"). Such techniques lower its non-U.S. tax rate to 2.3 per cent, while normally the corporate tax rate in, for instance, the UK is 28 per cent.[[243]](#cite_note-244) This reportedly sparked a French investigation into Google's [transfer pricing](/wiki/Transfer_pricing "Transfer pricing") practices in 2012.[[244]](#cite_note-245)

In 2020, Google said it had overhauled its controversial global tax structure and consolidated all of its intellectual property holdings back to the U.S.[[245]](#cite_note-246) Google Vice-president [Matt Brittin](/wiki/Matt_Brittin "Matt Brittin") testified to the [Public Accounts Committee](/wiki/Public_Accounts_Committee_(United_Kingdom) "Public Accounts Committee (United Kingdom)") of the [UK House of Commons](/wiki/UK_House_of_Commons "UK House of Commons") that his UK sales team made no sales and hence owed no sales taxes to the UK.[[246]](#cite_note-247) In January 2016, Google reached a settlement with the UK to pay £130m in back taxes plus higher taxes in future.[[247]](#cite_note-248) In 2017, Google channeled $22.7 billion from the Netherlands to Bermuda to reduce its tax bill.[[248]](#cite_note-249) In 2013, Google ranked 5th in [lobbying](/wiki/Lobbying_in_the_United_States "Lobbying in the United States") spending, up from 213th in 2003. In 2012, the company ranked 2nd in campaign donations of technology and Internet sections.[[249]](#cite_note-lobby1-250)

### Corporate identity

Further information: [History of Google § Name](/wiki/History_of_Google#Name "History of Google"), [Google (verb)](/wiki/Google_(verb) "Google (verb)"), [Google logo](/wiki/Google_logo "Google logo"), [Google Doodle](/wiki/Google_Doodle "Google Doodle"), [List of Google April Fools' Day jokes](/wiki/List_of_Google_April_Fools%27_Day_jokes "List of Google April Fools' Day jokes"), and [List of Google Easter eggs](/wiki/List_of_Google_Easter_eggs "List of Google Easter eggs")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Google_logo_%282013-2015%29.svg/250px-Google_logo_%282013-2015%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Google_logo_(2013-2015).svg)

Google's logo from 2013 to 2015. The logo had been used with minor changes since 1999.

The name "Google" originated from a misspelling of "[googol](/wiki/Googol "Googol")",[[250]](#cite_note-251)[[39]](#cite_note-Hanley,_Rachael-40) which refers to the number represented by a 1 followed by one-hundred zeros. Page and Brin write in their original paper on [PageRank](/wiki/PageRank "PageRank"):[[35]](#cite_note-originalpaper-36) "We chose our system name, Google, because it is a common spelling of googol, or 10100[,] and fits well with our goal of building very large-scale search engines." Having found its way increasingly into everyday language, the verb "google" was added to the *[Merriam Webster Collegiate Dictionary](/wiki/Merriam-Webster_Collegiate_Dictionary "Merriam-Webster Collegiate Dictionary")* and the *[Oxford English Dictionary](/wiki/Oxford_English_Dictionary "Oxford English Dictionary")* in 2006, meaning "to use the Google search engine to obtain information on the Internet."[[251]](#cite_note-252)[[67]](#cite_note-google_or_not-68) Google's [mission statement](/wiki/Mission_statement "Mission statement"), from the outset, was "to organize the world's information and make it universally accessible and useful",[[252]](#cite_note-253) and its unofficial slogan is "[Don't be evil](/wiki/Don%27t_be_evil "Don't be evil")".[[253]](#cite_note-254) In October 2015, a related motto was adopted in the Alphabet corporate code of conduct by the phrase: "Do the right thing".[[254]](#cite_note-255) The original motto was retained in the code of conduct of Google, now a subsidiary of Alphabet.

The original Google logo was designed by Sergey Brin.[[255]](#cite_note-256) Since 1998,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Google&action=edit) Google has been designing special, temporary alternate logos to place on their homepage intended to celebrate holidays, events, achievements and people. The first [Google Doodle](/wiki/Google_Doodle "Google Doodle") was in honor of the [Burning Man Festival](/wiki/Burning_Man "Burning Man") of 1998.[[256]](#cite_note-257)[[257]](#cite_note-258) The doodle was designed by Larry Page and Sergey Brin to notify users of their absence in case the servers crashed. Subsequent Google Doodles were designed by an outside contractor, until Larry and Sergey asked then-[intern](/wiki/Intern "Intern") [Dennis Hwang](/wiki/Dennis_Hwang "Dennis Hwang") to design a logo for [Bastille Day](/wiki/Bastille_Day "Bastille Day") in 2000. From that point onward, Doodles have been organized and created by a team of employees termed "Doodlers".[[258]](#cite_note-259)

Google has a tradition of creating [April Fools' Day](/wiki/April_Fools%27_Day "April Fools' Day") jokes. Its first on April 1, 2000, was [Google MentalPlex](/wiki/Google%27s_hoaxes#2000 "Google's hoaxes") which allegedly featured the use of mental power to search the web.[[259]](#cite_note-mentalplex-260) In 2007, Google announced a free Internet service called [TiSP](/wiki/TiSP "TiSP"), or Toilet Internet Service Provider, where one obtained a connection by flushing one end of a [fiber-optic](/wiki/Optical_fiber "Optical fiber") cable down their toilet.[[260]](#cite_note-TiSP-261) Google's services contain [easter eggs](/wiki/Easter_egg_(media) "Easter egg (media)"), such as the [Swedish Chef](/wiki/Swedish_Chef "Swedish Chef")'s "Bork bork bork", [Pig Latin](/wiki/Pig_Latin "Pig Latin"), "Hacker" or [leetspeak](/wiki/Leet "Leet"), [Elmer Fudd](/wiki/Elmer_Fudd "Elmer Fudd"), [Pirate](/wiki/International_Talk_Like_a_Pirate_Day "International Talk Like a Pirate Day"), and [Klingon](/wiki/Klingon_language "Klingon language") as language selections for its search engine.[[261]](#cite_note-262) When searching for the word "[anagram](/wiki/Anagram "Anagram")", meaning a rearrangement of letters from one word to form other valid words, Google's suggestion feature displays "Did you mean: nag a ram?"[[262]](#cite_note-263) Since 2019, Google runs free online courses to help engineers learn how to plan and author [technical documentation](/wiki/Technical_documentation "Technical documentation") better.[[263]](#cite_note-264)

### Workplace culture

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Pride_in_London_2016_-_Google_participating_in_the_parade.png/250px-Pride_in_London_2016_-_Google_participating_in_the_parade.png)](/wiki/File:Pride_in_London_2016_-_Google_participating_in_the_parade.png)

Google employees marching in the [Pride in London](/wiki/Pride_in_London "Pride in London") parade in 2016

On *[Fortune](/wiki/Fortune_(magazine) "Fortune (magazine)")* magazine's list of the best companies to work for, Google ranked first in 2007, 2008 and 2012,[[264]](#cite_note-best_company-265)[[265]](#cite_note-266)[[266]](#cite_note-267) and fourth in 2009 and 2010.[[267]](#cite_note-268)[[268]](#cite_note-269) Google was also nominated in 2010 to be the world's most attractive employer to graduating students in the Universum Communications talent attraction index.[[269]](#cite_note-270) Google's corporate philosophy includes principles such as "you can make money without doing evil", "you can be serious without a suit", and "work should be challenging and the challenge should be fun".[[270]](#cite_note-271)

As of September 30, 2020,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Google&action=edit) Alphabet Inc. had 132,121 employees,[[271]](#cite_note-272) of which more than 100,000 worked for Google.[[8]](#cite_note-Wakabayashi2019-9) Google's 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Google&action=edit) diversity report states that 32 percent of its workforce are women and 68 percent are men, with the ethnicity of its workforce being predominantly white (51.7%) and Asian (41.9%).[[272]](#cite_note-273) Within tech roles, 23.6 percent were women; and 26.7 percent of leadership roles were held by women.[[273]](#cite_note-274) In addition to its 100,000+ full-time employees, Google used about 121,000 temporary workers and contractors, as of March 2019.[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Google&action=edit)[[8]](#cite_note-Wakabayashi2019-9)

Google's employees are hired based on a hierarchical system. Employees are split into six hierarchies based on experience and can range "from entry-level data center workers at level one to managers and experienced engineers at level six".[[274]](#cite_note-275) As a motivation technique, Google uses a policy known as [Innovation Time Off](/wiki/Innovation_Time_Off "Innovation Time Off"), where Google engineers are encouraged to spend 20% of their work time on projects that interest them. Some of Google's services, such as Gmail, [Google News](/wiki/Google_News "Google News"), [Orkut](/wiki/Orkut "Orkut"), and [AdSense](/wiki/AdSense "AdSense"), originated from these independent endeavors.[[275]](#cite_note-276) In a talk at Stanford University, [Marissa Mayer](/wiki/Marissa_Mayer "Marissa Mayer"), Google's vice-president of Search Products and User Experience until July 2012, showed that half of all new product launches in the second half of 2005 had originated from the Innovation Time Off.[[276]](#cite_note-277)

In 2005, articles in *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")*[[277]](#cite_note-278) and other sources began suggesting that Google had lost its anti-corporate, no evil philosophy.[[278]](#cite_note-279)[[279]](#cite_note-280)[[280]](#cite_note-281) In an effort to maintain the company's unique culture, Google designated a Chief Culture Officer whose purpose was to develop and maintain the culture and work on ways to keep true to the core values that the company was founded on.[[281]](#cite_note-CCO-282) Google has also faced allegations of [sexism](/wiki/Sexism "Sexism") and [ageism](/wiki/Ageism "Ageism") from former employees.[[282]](#cite_note-283)[[283]](#cite_note-284) In 2013, a [class action against](/wiki/High-Tech_Employee_Antitrust_Litigation "High-Tech Employee Antitrust Litigation") several [Silicon Valley](/wiki/Silicon_Valley "Silicon Valley") companies, including Google, was filed for alleged "no cold call" agreements which restrained the recruitment of high-tech employees.[[284]](#cite_note-285) In a lawsuit filed January 8, 2018, multiple employees and job applicants alleged Google discriminated against a class defined by their "conservative political views[,] male gender[,] and/or ... Caucasian or Asian race".[[285]](#cite_note-DhillonLaw-286)

On January 25, 2020, the formation of an international workers union of Google employees, Alpha Global, was announced.[[286]](#cite_note-287) The coalition is made up of "13 different unions representing workers in 10 countries, including the United States, [the] United Kingdom, and Switzerland".[[287]](#cite_note-Schiffer2021-288) The group is affiliated with the [UNI Global Union](/wiki/UNI_Global_Union "UNI Global Union"), which represents nearly 20 million international workers from various unions and federations. The formation of the union is in response to persistent allegations of mistreatment of Google employees and a toxic workplace culture.[[287]](#cite_note-Schiffer2021-288)[[288]](#cite_note-289)[[285]](#cite_note-DhillonLaw-286) Google had previously been accused of surveilling and firing employees who were suspected of organizing a workers union.[[289]](#cite_note-290) In 2021, court documents revealed that between 2018 and 2020, Google ran an anti-union campaign called Project Vivian to "convince them (employees) that unions suck".[[290]](#cite_note-unions-291) In February 2025, Google dropped their commitment to make "[diversity, equity, and inclusion](/wiki/Diversity,_equity,_and_inclusion "Diversity, equity, and inclusion") [DEI] part of everything we do" from their annual investor report. This action followed Meta, Amazon, Pepsi, McDonald's, Walmart, and others who all have rolled back their DEI programmes.[[291]](#cite_note-292)

### Office locations

Further information: [Googleplex](/wiki/Googleplex "Googleplex")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/111_Eighth_Avenue.jpg/250px-111_Eighth_Avenue.jpg)](/wiki/File:111_Eighth_Avenue.jpg)

Google's New York City office building houses its largest advertising sales team.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Google_at_111_Richmond_Street_West_in_Toronto_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Google_at_111_Richmond_Street_West_in_Toronto_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Google_at_111_Richmond_Street_West_in_Toronto_(cropped).jpg)

Google's [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto") office

Google's headquarters in Mountain View, California is referred to as "the Googleplex", a play on words on the number [googolplex](/wiki/Googolplex "Googolplex") and the headquarters itself being a *complex* of buildings. Internationally, Google has over 78 offices in more than 50 countries.[[292]](#cite_note-293)

In 2006, Google moved into about 300,000 square feet (27,900 m2) of office space at [111 Eighth Avenue](/wiki/111_Eighth_Avenue "111 Eighth Avenue") in [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan"), [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"). The office houses its largest advertising sales team.[[293]](#cite_note-294) In 2010, Google bought the building housing the headquarters, in a deal that valued the property at around $1.9 billion.[[294]](#cite_note-295)[[295]](#cite_note-296) In March 2018, Google's parent company Alphabet bought the nearby [Chelsea Market](/wiki/Chelsea_Market "Chelsea Market") building for $2.4 billion. The sale is touted as one of the most expensive real estate transactions for a single building in the history of New York.[[296]](#cite_note-297)[[297]](#cite_note-298)[[298]](#cite_note-299)[[299]](#cite_note-300) In November 2018, Google announced its plan to expand its New York City office to a capacity of 12,000 employees.[[300]](#cite_note-301) The same December, it was announced that a $1 billion, 1,700,000-square-foot (160,000 m2) headquarters for Google would be built in Manhattan's [Hudson Square](/wiki/Hudson_Square "Hudson Square") neighborhood.[[301]](#cite_note-302)[[302]](#cite_note-303) Called Google Hudson Square, the new campus is projected to more than double the number of Google employees working in New York City.[[303]](#cite_note-304)

By late 2006, Google established a new headquarters for its AdWords division in [Ann Arbor, Michigan](/wiki/Ann_Arbor,_Michigan "Ann Arbor, Michigan").[[304]](#cite_note-305) In November 2006, Google opened offices on [Carnegie Mellon](/wiki/Carnegie_Mellon "Carnegie Mellon")'s campus in [Pittsburgh](/wiki/Pittsburgh "Pittsburgh"), focusing on shopping-related advertisement coding and [smartphone applications](/wiki/Smartphone_applications "Smartphone applications") and programs.[[305]](#cite_note-306)[[306]](#cite_note-307) Other office locations in the U.S. include [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta,_Georgia "Atlanta, Georgia"); [Austin](/wiki/Austin,_Texas "Austin, Texas"); [Boulder, Colorado](/wiki/Boulder,_Colorado "Boulder, Colorado"); [Cambridge, Massachusetts](/wiki/Cambridge,_Massachusetts "Cambridge, Massachusetts"); [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco"); [Seattle](/wiki/Seattle,_Washington "Seattle, Washington") and [Kirkland, Washington](/wiki/Kirkland,_Washington "Kirkland, Washington"); [Birmingham, Michigan](/wiki/Birmingham,_Michigan "Birmingham, Michigan"); [Reston, Virginia](/wiki/Reston,_Virginia "Reston, Virginia"), [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."),[[307]](#cite_note-308) and [Madison, Wisconsin](/wiki/Madison,_Wisconsin "Madison, Wisconsin").[[308]](#cite_note-309)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Google_Headquarters_in_Ireland_Building_Sign.jpg/250px-Google_Headquarters_in_Ireland_Building_Sign.jpg)](/wiki/File:Google_Headquarters_in_Ireland_Building_Sign.jpg)

Google's Dublin Ireland office, headquarters of Google Ads for Europe

It also has product research and development operations in cities around the world, namely [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney") (birthplace location of Google Maps)[[309]](#cite_note-310) and London (part of Android development).[[310]](#cite_note-311) In November 2013, Google announced plans for a new London headquarter, a 1 million square foot office able to accommodate 4,500 employees. Recognized as one of the biggest ever commercial property acquisitions at the time of the deal's announcement in January,[[311]](#cite_note-312) Google submitted plans for the new headquarter to the [Camden Council](/wiki/Camden_London_Borough_Council "Camden London Borough Council") in June 2017.[[312]](#cite_note-313)[[313]](#cite_note-314)

In May 2015, Google announced its intention to create its own campus in [Hyderabad](/wiki/Hyderabad "Hyderabad"), India. The new campus, reported to be the company's largest outside the United States, will accommodate 13,000 employees.[[314]](#cite_note-315)[[315]](#cite_note-316) In September 2025 Google opened their £735m [AI](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence "Artificial intelligence") Centre in [Waltham Cross, Hertfordshire](/wiki/Waltham_Cross "Waltham Cross") and announced their plans for £5 bn investment in AI research, in the same month that [Alphabet](/wiki/Alphabet_Inc. "Alphabet Inc.") reached market capitalisation of $3 [trillion](/wiki/Trillion "Trillion").[[316]](#cite_note-317)[[317]](#cite_note-318)

Google's Global Offices sum a total of 86 locations worldwide,[[318]](#cite_note-Google_world_office_locations-319) with 32 offices in North America, three of them in Canada and 29 in the United States, California being the state with the most Google's offices with 9 in total including the Googleplex. Google counts 6 offices in the [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America") region and 24 in Europe, 3 of them in United Kingdom. The [Asia-Pacific](/wiki/Asia-Pacific "Asia-Pacific") region counts with 26 offices principally five in India and three in Australia, and three in China, while the Africa and [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East") region counts five offices.

### Infrastructure

Further information: [Google data centers](/wiki/Google_data_centers "Google data centers")

Google has data centers in [North](/wiki/North_America "North America") and [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America"), [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"), and [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe").[[320]](#cite_note-321) There is no official data on the number of [servers](/wiki/Server_(computing) "Server (computing)") in Google data centers; however, research and advisory firm [Gartner](/wiki/Gartner "Gartner") estimated in a July 2016 report that Google at the time had 2.5 million servers.[[321]](#cite_note-322) Traditionally, Google relied on [parallel computing](/wiki/Parallel_computing "Parallel computing") on commodity hardware like mainstream [x86](/wiki/X86 "X86") computers (similar to home PCs) to keep costs per query low.[[322]](#cite_note-323)[[323]](#cite_note-324)[[324]](#cite_note-CNET2009-325) In 2005, it started developing its own designs, which were only revealed in 2009.[[324]](#cite_note-CNET2009-325)

Google has built its own private [submarine communications cables](/wiki/Submarine_communications_cable "Submarine communications cable"). The first cable, named Curie, connects California with [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile") and was completed on November 15, 2019.[[325]](#cite_note-326)[[326]](#cite_note-327) The second fully Google-owned undersea cable, named Dunant, connects the United States with France and is planned to begin operation in 2020.[[327]](#cite_note-328) Google's third subsea cable, Equiano, will connect [Lisbon](/wiki/Lisbon "Lisbon") ([Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal")) with [Lagos](/wiki/Lagos "Lagos") ([Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria")) and [Cape Town](/wiki/Cape_Town "Cape Town") ([South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa")).[[328]](#cite_note-329) The company's fourth cable, named Grace Hopper, connects landing points in [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") (US), [Bude](/wiki/Bude "Bude") ([UK](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom")) and [Bilbao](/wiki/Bilbao "Bilbao") ([Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain")), and is expected to become operational in 2022.[[329]](#cite_note-330)

### Environment

In October 2006, the company announced plans to install thousands of [solar panels](/wiki/Solar_panel "Solar panel") on its Mountain View campus to provide up to 1.6 [Megawatt](/wiki/MegaWatt "MegaWatt") of electricity, enough to satisfy approximately 30% of the campus' energy needs.[[330]](#cite_note-solar-331)[[331]](#cite_note-332) The system is the largest [rooftop photovoltaic power station](/wiki/Rooftop_photovoltaic_power_station "Rooftop photovoltaic power station") constructed on a U.S. corporate campus and one of the largest on any corporate site in the world.[[330]](#cite_note-solar-331) Since 2007,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Google&action=edit) Google has aimed for [carbon neutrality](/wiki/Carbon_neutrality "Carbon neutrality") in regard to its operations.[[332]](#cite_note-333) In Spring 2009, Google hired a herd of 200 [goats](/wiki/Goat "Goat") for a week from California Grazing to mow their lawn. It was apparently more eco-friendly.[[333]](#cite_note-334)

Google disclosed in September 2011 that it "continuously uses enough electricity to power 200,000 homes", almost 260 million watts or about a quarter of the output of a [nuclear power plant](/wiki/Nuclear_power_plant "Nuclear power plant"). Total carbon emissions for 2010 were just under 1.5 million metric tons, mostly due to fossil fuels that provide electricity for the data centers. Google said that 25 percent of its energy was supplied by renewable fuels in 2010. An average search uses only 0.3 watt-hours of electricity, so all global searches are only 12.5 million watts or 5% of the total electricity consumption by Google.[[334]](#cite_note-335)

In 2010, [Google Energy](/wiki/Google_Energy "Google Energy") made its first investment in a [renewable energy](/wiki/Renewable_energy "Renewable energy") project, putting $38.8 million into two [wind farms](/wiki/Wind_farm "Wind farm") in [North Dakota](/wiki/North_Dakota "North Dakota"). The company announced the two locations will generate 169.5 megawatts of power, enough to supply 55,000 homes.[[335]](#cite_note-336) In February 2010, the [Federal Energy Regulatory Commission](/wiki/Federal_Energy_Regulatory_Commission "Federal Energy Regulatory Commission") granted Google an authorization to buy and sell energy at market rates.[[336]](#cite_note-337) The corporation exercised this authorization in September 2013 when it announced it would purchase all the electricity produced by the not-yet-built 240-megawatt Happy Hereford wind farm.[[337]](#cite_note-338) In July 2010, Google signed an agreement with an [Iowa](/wiki/Iowa "Iowa") wind farm to buy 114 megawatts of power for 20 years.[[338]](#cite_note-wind_energy-339)

In December 2016, Google announced that—starting in 2017—it would purchase enough renewable energy to match 100% of the energy usage of its data centers and offices. The commitment will make Google "the world's largest corporate buyer of renewable power, with commitments reaching 2.6 gigawatts (2,600 megawatts) of wind and solar energy".[[339]](#cite_note-340)[[340]](#cite_note-341)[[341]](#cite_note-342) In November 2017, Google bought 536 megawatts of wind power. The purchase made the firm reach [100% renewable energy](/wiki/100%25_renewable_energy "100% renewable energy"). The wind energy comes from two power plants in [South Dakota](/wiki/South_Dakota "South Dakota"), one in Iowa and one in [Oklahoma](/wiki/Oklahoma "Oklahoma").[[342]](#cite_note-343) In September 2019, Google's chief executive announced plans for a $2 billion wind and solar investment, the biggest renewable energy deal in corporate history. This will grow their green energy profile by 40%, giving them an extra 1.6 gigawatt of clean energy, the company said.[[343]](#cite_note-344)

In September 2020, Google announced it had retroactively offset all of its carbon emissions since the company's foundation in 1998.[[344]](#cite_note-345) It also stated that it is committed to operating its data centers and offices using only carbon-free energy by 2030.[[345]](#cite_note-346) In October 2020, the company pledged to make the packaging for its hardware products 100% plastic-free and 100% recyclable by 2025. It also said that all its final assembly manufacturing sites will achieve a [UL](/wiki/UL_(safety_organization) "UL (safety organization)") 2799 [Zero Waste to Landfill](/wiki/Zero_waste#Corporate_initiatives "Zero waste") certification by 2022 by ensuring that the vast majority of waste from the manufacturing process is recycled instead of ending up in a landfill.[[346]](#cite_note-347) In 2023 Google consumed 24 TWh of electricity, more than countries such as Iceland, Ghana, the Dominican Republic, or Tunisia.[[347]](#cite_note-348)

#### Climate change denial and misinformation

Google donates to [climate change denial](/wiki/Climate_change_denial "Climate change denial") political groups including the [State Policy Network](/wiki/State_Policy_Network "State Policy Network") and the [Competitive Enterprise Institute](/wiki/Competitive_Enterprise_Institute "Competitive Enterprise Institute").[[348]](#cite_note-349)[[349]](#cite_note-350) The company also actively funds and profits from climate [disinformation](/wiki/Disinformation "Disinformation") by monetizing ad spaces on most of the largest climate disinformation sites.[[350]](#cite_note-351) Google continued to monetize and profit from sites propagating climate disinformation even after the company updated their policy to prohibit placing their ads on similar sites.[[351]](#cite_note-352)

### Philanthropy

Main article: [Google.org](/wiki/Google.org "Google.org")

In 2004, Google formed the not-for-profit philanthropic Google.org, with a start-up fund of $1 billion.[[352]](#cite_note-philanthropy-353) The mission of the organization is to create [awareness about climate change](/wiki/Climate_change_education "Climate change education"), global public health, and global poverty. One of its first projects was to develop a viable [plug-in hybrid](/wiki/Plug-in_hybrid "Plug-in hybrid") [electric vehicle](/wiki/Electric_vehicle "Electric vehicle") that can attain 100 miles per gallon. Google hired [Larry Brilliant](/wiki/Larry_Brilliant "Larry Brilliant") as the program's executive director in 2004[[353]](#cite_note-354) and Megan Smith has since[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Google&action=edit) replaced him as director.[[354]](#cite_note-355)

In March 2007, in partnership with the [Mathematical Sciences Research Institute](/wiki/Mathematical_Sciences_Research_Institute "Mathematical Sciences Research Institute") (MSRI), Google hosted the first [Julia Robinson Mathematics Festival](/wiki/Julia_Robinson_Mathematics_Festival "Julia Robinson Mathematics Festival") at its headquarters in Mountain View.[[355]](#cite_note-356) In 2011, Google donated €1 million to [International Mathematical Olympiad](/wiki/International_Mathematical_Olympiad "International Mathematical Olympiad") to support the next five annual International Mathematical Olympiads (2011–2015).[[356]](#cite_note-357)[[357]](#cite_note-358) In July 2012, Google launched a "[Legalize Love](/wiki/Legalize_Love "Legalize Love")" campaign in support of [gay rights](/wiki/Gay_rights "Gay rights").[[358]](#cite_note-359)

In 2008, Google announced its "project 10100", which accepted ideas for how to help the community and then allowed Google users to vote on their favorites.[[359]](#cite_note-360) After two years of no update, during which many wondered what had happened to the program,[[360]](#cite_note-361) Google revealed the winners of the project, giving a total of ten million dollars to various ideas ranging from non-profit organizations that promote education to a website that intends to make all legal documents public and online.[[361]](#cite_note-362) Responding to the humanitarian crisis after the [2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine"), Google announced a $15 million donation to support Ukrainian citizens.[[362]](#cite_note-363) The company also decided to transform its office in Warsaw into a help center for refugees.[[363]](#cite_note-364) Also in February 2022, Google announced a $100 million fund to expand skills training and job placement for low-income Americans, in conjunction with non-profits [Year Up](/wiki/Year_Up "Year Up"), [Social Finance](/wiki/Social_Finance_(consultancy) "Social Finance (consultancy)"), and Merit America.[[364]](#cite_note-365)

### Lobbying and political influence

In 2025, Google was one of the donors who funded the White House's [East Wing demolition](/wiki/East_Wing#2025_demolition "East Wing"), and planned building of a ballroom.[[365]](#cite_note-366)

## Criticism and controversies

Further information: [Criticism of Google](/wiki/Criticism_of_Google "Criticism of Google") and [Google litigation](/wiki/Google_litigation "Google litigation")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Google_bus_protest.jpg/250px-Google_bus_protest.jpg)](/wiki/File:Google_bus_protest.jpg)

San Francisco activists [protest privately owned shuttle buses](/wiki/San_Francisco_tech_bus_protests "San Francisco tech bus protests") that transport workers for tech companies such as Google from their homes in San Francisco and Oakland to corporate campuses in Silicon Valley.

Google has had criticism over issues such as [aggressive tax avoidance](/wiki/Google_tax_avoidance "Google tax avoidance"),[[366]](#cite_note-367) [search neutrality](/wiki/Search_neutrality "Search neutrality"), [copyright](/wiki/Copyright "Copyright"), [censorship](/wiki/Censorship_by_Google "Censorship by Google") of search results and content,[[367]](#cite_note-368) and [privacy](/wiki/Google_privacy "Google privacy").[[368]](#cite_note-369)[[369]](#cite_note-370) Other criticisms are alleged misuse and manipulation of search results, its use of other people's [intellectual property](/wiki/Intellectual_property "Intellectual property"), concerns that its [compilation of data](/wiki/Data_collection "Data collection") may violate [Internet privacy](/wiki/Internet_privacy "Internet privacy"), and the [energy consumption](/wiki/Energy_consumption "Energy consumption") of its servers, as well as concerns over traditional business issues such as [monopoly](/wiki/Monopoly "Monopoly"), [restraint of trade](/wiki/Restraint_of_trade "Restraint of trade"), [anti-competitive practices](/wiki/Anti-competitive_practices "Anti-competitive practices"), and [patent infringement](/wiki/Patent_infringement "Patent infringement"). When Google's parent company [Alphabet](/wiki/Alphabet_Inc. "Alphabet Inc.") announced in September 2025 that it would reinstate YouTube creators that were banned for spreading [misinformation about COVID-19](/wiki/Misinformation_about_COVID-19 "Misinformation about COVID-19") and the [2020 U.S. presidential election](/wiki/2020_U.S._presidential_election "2020 U.S. presidential election"),[[370]](#cite_note-371) it was criticized for prioritizing "free expression" over "facts" and placed within the context of the company's shift dating back to 2023.[[371]](#cite_note-372)

### Political controversies

#### United States

In a 2022 [National Labor Relations Board](/wiki/National_Labor_Relations_Board "National Labor Relations Board") ruling, court documents suggested that Google sponsored a secretive project—*Project Vivian*—to counsel its employees and to discourage them from forming unions.[[290]](#cite_note-unions-291)

#### Brazil

On May 1, 2023, Google placed an ad against the [Brazilian Congressional Bill No. 2630](/wiki/Brazilian_Congressional_Bill_No._2630 "Brazilian Congressional Bill No. 2630"), an anti-[disinformation](/wiki/Disinformation "Disinformation") law that was about to be approved, on its search homepage in Brazil, calling on its users to ask congressional representatives to oppose the legislation. The country's government and judiciary accused the company of undue interference in the congressional debate, saying it could amount to abuse of economic power and ordering the company to change the ad within two hours of notification or face fines of [R$](/wiki/Brazilian_real "Brazilian real")1 million (2023) ([US$](/wiki/USD "USD")185,528.76) per non-compliance hour. The company then promptly removed the ad.[[372]](#cite_note-373)[[373]](#cite_note-374)

#### Israeli–Palestinian conflict

Further information: [Israeli–Palestinian conflict](/wiki/Israeli%E2%80%93Palestinian_conflict "Israeli–Palestinian conflict")

Google has a US$1.2 billion artificial intelligence and [surveillance](/wiki/Surveillance "Surveillance") contract with the [Israeli military](/wiki/Israeli_military "Israeli military") known as [Project Nimbus](/wiki/Project_Nimbus "Project Nimbus"). According to Google employees, the Israeli military could use this technology to expand its surveillance of [Palestinians](/wiki/Palestinians "Palestinians") living in the [occupied territories](/wiki/Israeli-occupied_territories "Israeli-occupied territories").[[374]](#cite_note-375) Google relocated an outspoken employee overseas, and the employee claimed it was a "retaliation for publicly criticizing the contract".[[375]](#cite_note-376) Other Palestinian employees have described an "institutionalised bias" within the company.[[376]](#cite_note-377)

In 2021, Google and Amazon engaged in negotiations for a substantial cloud computing agreement valued at $1.2 billion, during which Israel insisted on the inclusion of a confidential code referred to as the "blink mechanism". This stipulation compelled Google and Amazon to essentially disregard legal responsibilities in various nations. Israel expressed apprehension that the data transferred to the cloud services of these global corporations might be accessible to foreign law enforcement agencies. As per documents disclosed to The Guardian, both Google and Amazon consented to the blink mechanism in order to finalize the profitable agreement.[[377]](#cite_note-378)

During 2025, Google engaged in a $45 million, six-month contract with Israel to run advertising campaigns. Some Youtube ads aimed to cast doubt on the existence of a famine in [Gaza](/wiki/Gaza_Strip "Gaza Strip"). Many complaints were filed against Israel's videos, but Google maintained that the ads did not violate its content policies.[[378]](#cite_note-379)

#### Russia

On October 31, 2024, the [Russian government](/wiki/Russian_government "Russian government") imposed a "symbolic" fine of $20 [decillion](/wiki/Decillion "Decillion") on Google for blocking pro-Russian YouTube channels. In 2022, during the invasion of Ukraine, a Russian court had ordered Google to restore the channels, with penalties doubling every week according to [TASS](/wiki/TASS "TASS").[[379]](#cite_note-380) This comes alongside other large fines against social media companies accused of hosting content critical of the Kremlin or supportive of Ukraine.[[380]](#cite_note-381)

### Antitrust

See also: [List of mergers and acquisitions by Alphabet](/wiki/List_of_mergers_and_acquisitions_by_Alphabet "List of mergers and acquisitions by Alphabet")

In July 2018, [Mozilla](/wiki/Mozilla "Mozilla") program manager Chris Peterson accused Google of intentionally slowing down YouTube performance on [Firefox](/wiki/Firefox "Firefox").[[381]](#cite_note-382)[[382]](#cite_note-firefox-383) In April 2019, former Mozilla executive Jonathan Nightingale accused Google of intentionally and systematically sabotaging the Firefox browser over the past decade in order to boost adoption of Google Chrome.[[382]](#cite_note-firefox-383) In 2019, a hub for critics of Google dedicated to abstaining from using Google products coalesced in the [Reddit](/wiki/Reddit "Reddit") online [community](/wiki/Subreddit "Subreddit") /r/degoogle.[[383]](#cite_note-384) The [DeGoogle](/wiki/DeGoogle "DeGoogle") [grassroots campaign](/wiki/Grassroots_campaign "Grassroots campaign") continues to grow as privacy activists highlight information about Google products, and the associated incursion on personal privacy rights by the company. Google reportedly paid Apple $22 billion in 2022 to maintain its position as the default search engine on Safari. It marks one of the largest payments between two tech giants in recent years.[[384]](#cite_note-385)

#### European Union

Main article: [Antitrust cases against Google by the European Union](/wiki/Antitrust_cases_against_Google_by_the_European_Union "Antitrust cases against Google by the European Union")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Belgique_-_Bruxelles_-_Schuman_-_Berlaymont_-_01.jpg/250px-Belgique_-_Bruxelles_-_Schuman_-_Berlaymont_-_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Belgique_-_Bruxelles_-_Schuman_-_Berlaymont_-_01.jpg)

The European Commission, which imposed three fines on Google in 2017, 2018, and 2019

On June 27, 2017, the company received a record fine of [€](/wiki/Euro "Euro")2.42 billion from the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") (EU) for "promoting its own shopping comparison service at the top of search results".[[385]](#cite_note-386) On July 18, 2018, the [European Commissioner for Competition](/wiki/European_Commissioner_for_Competition "European Commissioner for Competition") fined Google €4.34 billion for breaching [EU antitrust](/w/index.php?title=EU_antitrust&action=edit&redlink=1 "EU antitrust (page does not exist)") rules. The abuse of dominants position has been referred to as Google's constraint applied to Android device manufacturers and network operators to ensure that traffic on Android devices goes to the Google search engine.[[386]](#cite_note-387) On October 9, 2018, Google confirmed that it had appealed the fine to the [General Court](/wiki/General_Court_(European_Union) "General Court (European Union)") of the EU.[[387]](#cite_note-388)[[388]](#cite_note-389)

On March 20, 2019, the [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission") imposed a €1.49 billion ($1.69 billion) fine on Google for preventing rivals from being able to "compete and innovate fairly" in the online advertising market. EU competition commissioner [Margrethe Vestager](/wiki/Margrethe_Vestager "Margrethe Vestager") said Google had violated EU antitrust rules by "imposing anti-competitive contractual restrictions on third-party websites" that required them to exclude search results from Google's rivals.[[389]](#cite_note-390)[[390]](#cite_note-391) On September 14, 2022, Google lost the appeal of a €4.125 billion (£3.5 billion) fine, which was ruled to be paid after it was proved by the European Commission that Google forced Android phone-makers to carry Google's search and web browser apps. Since the initial accusations, Google has changed its policy.[[391]](#cite_note-392)

In March 2024, a former Google [software engineer](/wiki/Software_engineer "Software engineer") and Chinese national named Linwei Ding was accused of stealing confidential artificial intelligence information from the company and handing it to Chinese corporations.[[392]](#cite_note-393) Ding had allegedly stolen over 500 files from the company over the course of 5 years, having been hired in 2019.[[393]](#cite_note-394) Upon discovering Ding had been in contact with Chinese state-owned companies, Google notified the [FBI](/wiki/FBI "FBI"), who carried on the investigation of the [data breach](/wiki/Data_breach "Data breach").[[394]](#cite_note-395) On September 10, 2024, Europe's top court imposed a €2.4 billion fine on Google for abusing its dominance in the shopping comparison market, marking the conclusion of a case that began in 2009 with a complaint from British firm Foundem.[[395]](#cite_note-396)

On September 18, 2024, Alphabet's Google won a €1.49 billion ($1.7 billion) antitrust fine from the EU, while Qualcomm's efforts to repeal a penalty were unsuccessful. The General Court agreed with many of the European Commission's findings but annulled the Google fine, stating that the Commission failed to consider all relevant factors and did not demonstrate harm to innovation or consumers. Google noted that it had already changed its contract practices in 2016. Meanwhile, Qualcomm saw its fine reduced slightly but failed to overturn the ruling regarding its predatory pricing against Icera. Both companies have options to appeal further.[[396]](#cite_note-397) On September 5, 2025, the European Commission fined Google €2.95 billion ($3.47 billion), for breaching EU antitrust rules. Regulators say Google abused its dominance by giving preferential treatment to its [ad exchange](/wiki/Ad_exchange "Ad exchange") within its publisher ad server and ad-buying tools.[[397]](#cite_note-398)

#### United States

After U.S. Congressional hearings in July 2020,[[398]](#cite_note-399) and a report from the U.S. House of Representatives' Antitrust Subcommittee released in early October,[[399]](#cite_note-400) the [U.S. Department of Justice](/wiki/U.S._Department_of_Justice "U.S. Department of Justice") filed an [antitrust lawsuit against Google](/wiki/United_States_v._Google_LLC_(2020) "United States v. Google LLC (2020)") on October 20, 2020, asserting that it has illegally maintained its monopoly position in web search and search advertising.[[400]](#cite_note-401)[[401]](#cite_note-402) The lawsuit alleged that Google engaged in anticompetitive behavior by paying Apple between $8 billion and $12 billion to be the default search engine on iPhones.[[402]](#cite_note-403) Later that month, both [Facebook](/wiki/Facebook "Facebook") and Alphabet agreed to "cooperate and assist one another" in the face of investigation into their online advertising practices.[[403]](#cite_note-404)[[404]](#cite_note-405) [Another suit was brought against Google in 2023](/wiki/United_States_v._Google_LLC_(2023) "United States v. Google LLC (2023)") for illegally monopolizing the advertising technology market.[[405]](#cite_note-406) In August 2024, [District of Columbia U.S. District Court](/wiki/District_of_Columbia_U.S._District_Court "District of Columbia U.S. District Court") Judge [Amit Mehta](/wiki/Amit_Mehta "Amit Mehta") ruled that Google held a monopoly in online search and text advertising in violation of Section 2 of the [Sherman Antitrust Act](/wiki/Sherman_Antitrust_Act "Sherman Antitrust Act").[[406]](#cite_note-407)[[407]](#cite_note-408)

On October 8, 2024, The U.S. government suggested it could request Google to divest parts of its business, such as the Chrome browser and Android, due to its alleged monopoly in online search. The Justice Department aimed to limit Google's growing dominance in areas like AI. Google, which intended to appeal, argued that the proposals were too extreme, while also dealing with other antitrust cases involving its app store and advertising operations.[[408]](#cite_note-409) In November 2024, the Justice Department proposed major changes to curb Google's online search monopoly, including forcing the company to sell its Chrome browser, share search data with competitors, and end exclusive agreements that make Google the default search engine on devices like iPhones. The DoJ also sought a ban on Google re-entering the browser market for five years and restrictions on its investments in rival search or AI technologies. Google called these proposals excessive and harmful to consumers, pledging to appeal. A trial on the case was scheduled for April 2025, though the incoming administration and new DoJ leadership could potentially alter the course of the proceedings.[[409]](#cite_note-410)

In September 2024, [Competition and Markets Authority](/wiki/Competition_and_Markets_Authority "Competition and Markets Authority") (CMA) provisionally found that Google engaged in anti-competitive practices in the online advertising technology market, potentially harming thousands of UK publishers and advertisers. The investigation claimed Google used its market power to prevent rivals from competing fairly, affecting billions spent on digital ads. Google rejected the findings as flawed, stating that its ad tech benefits businesses. If found guilty, Google could face penalties of up to 10% of its global turnover. Similar investigations are ongoing in the U.S. and EU, where regulators have suggested that Google may need to sell part of its ad-tech business.[[410]](#cite_note-411) It was ruled in 2025 by the Justice Department alongside 17 other states that Google operates a monopoly in online advertising technology. The case will now move to a remedies stage which may lead to Alphabet, the owner of Google, being broken up.[[411]](#cite_note-412)

### Gender discrimination lawsuit

In 2017, three women sued Google, accusing the company of violating California's Equal Pay Act by underpaying its female employees. The lawsuit cited the wage gap was around $17,000 and that Google locked women into lower career tracks, leading to smaller salaries and bonuses. In June 2022, Google agreed to pay a $118 million settlement to 15,550 female employees working in California since 2013. As a part of the settlement, Google also agreed to hire a third party to analyze its hiring and compensation practices.[[412]](#cite_note-413)[[413]](#cite_note-414)[[414]](#cite_note-415)

### Censorship

Main articles: [Censorship by Google](/wiki/Censorship_by_Google "Censorship by Google") and [Google China § Censorship](/wiki/Google_China#Censorship "Google China")

According to Ryan Gallagher of *[The Intercept](/wiki/The_Intercept "The Intercept")* in August 2018, Google was developing for the People's Republic of China a censored version of its search engine (known as [Dragonfly](/wiki/Dragonfly_(search_engine) "Dragonfly (search engine)")) "that will blacklist websites and search terms about human rights, democracy, religion, and peaceful protest".[[415]](#cite_note-416) Google was grilled at a [Senate Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation](/wiki/Senate_Committee_on_Commerce,_Science,_and_Transportation "Senate Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation") hearing on the project one month later.[[416]](#cite_note-417)[[417]](#cite_note-418) The project was canceled in December following the backlash it garnered both externally and internally within the company.[[418]](#cite_note-419)[[419]](#cite_note-420)

### Data loss

In May 2024, a misconfiguration in Google Cloud led to the [accidental deletion](/wiki/Data_loss "Data loss") of [UniSuper](/wiki/UniSuper "UniSuper")'s $135 billion Australian pension fund account, affecting over half a million members who were unable to access their accounts for a week. The outage, attributed to a cloud service error and not a cyberattack, prompted a joint apology from UniSuper and Google Cloud executives, who assured members that no personal data was compromised and restoration efforts were underway.[[420]](#cite_note-421)

### Data privacy

Further information: [Privacy concerns regarding Google](/wiki/Privacy_concerns_regarding_Google "Privacy concerns regarding Google") and [Corporate surveillance](/wiki/Corporate_surveillance "Corporate surveillance")

On October 8, 2018, a class action lawsuit was filed against Google and Alphabet due to "non-public" [Google+](/wiki/Google%2B "Google+") account data being exposed as a result of a bug that allowed app developers to gain access to the private information of users. The litigation was settled in July 2020 for $7.5 million with a payout to claimants of at least $5 each, with a maximum of $12 each.[[421]](#cite_note-422)[[422]](#cite_note-423)[[423]](#cite_note-424) On January 21, 2019, French data regulator [CNIL](/wiki/CNIL "CNIL") imposed a record €50 million fine on Google for breaching the European Union's [General Data Protection Regulation](/wiki/General_Data_Protection_Regulation "General Data Protection Regulation"). The judgment claimed Google had failed to sufficiently inform users of its methods for collecting data to personalize advertising. Google issued a statement saying it was "deeply committed" to transparency and was "studying the decision" before determining its response.[[424]](#cite_note-425) In November 2019, the Office for Civil Rights of the [Department of Health and Human Services](/wiki/Department_of_Health_and_Human_Services "Department of Health and Human Services") began investigation into [Project Nightingale](/wiki/Project_Nightingale "Project Nightingale"), to assess whether the "mass collection of individuals' medical records" complied with the [Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act](/wiki/Health_Insurance_Portability_and_Accountability_Act "Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act").[[425]](#cite_note-426) According to *The Wall Street Journal*, Google secretively began the project in 2018, with [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis "St. Louis")-based [healthcare](/wiki/Healthcare "Healthcare") company [Ascension](/wiki/Ascension_(company) "Ascension (company)").[[426]](#cite_note-427)

In early June 2020, a $5 billion class-action lawsuit was filed against Google by a group of consumers, alleging that Chrome's [Incognito browsing](/wiki/Private_browsing "Private browsing") mode still collects their user history.[[427]](#cite_note-428)[[428]](#cite_note-429) The lawsuit became known in March 2021 when a federal judge denied Google's request to dismiss the case, ruling that they must face the group's charges.[[429]](#cite_note-430)[[430]](#cite_note-431) [Reuters](/wiki/Reuters "Reuters") reported that the lawsuit alleged that Google's CEO [Sundar Pichai](/wiki/Sundar_Pichai "Sundar Pichai") sought to keep the users unaware of this issue.[[431]](#cite_note-432)
In April 2024, it was announced that Google agreed to settle this lawsuit. Under the terms of the settlement Google agreed to destroy billions of data records to settle a lawsuit claiming it secretly tracked the internet use of people who thought they were browsing privately.[[432]](#cite_note-433) On January 6, 2022, France's data privacy regulatory body CNIL fined Alphabet's Google 150 million euros (US$169 million) for not allowing its Internet users an easy refusal of [cookies](/wiki/HTTP_cookie "HTTP cookie") along with Facebook.[[433]](#cite_note-434)

In August 2024, Google sent an email to users informing them of its legal obligation to disclose certain confidential information to U.S. government authorities. The company stated that when it receives valid requests from government agencies to produce documents without redacting confidential customer information, it may produce such documents even if they are confidential to users; however, it will request confidential treatment of such information from the government.[[434]](#cite_note-435) In January 2025, U.S. federal judge [Richard Seeborg](/wiki/Richard_Seeborg "Richard Seeborg") rejected Google's motion to dismiss a [class-action](/wiki/Class_action "Class action") lawsuit. The lawsuit claims Google collected data from users who had specifically opted out of tracking.[[435]](#cite_note-436) In September 2025, a federal jury decided that Google must pay $425 million. Google said it would appeal the decision.[[436]](#cite_note-437)

#### Geolocation

Google has been criticized for continuing to [collect location data](/wiki/Location-based_service "Location-based service") from users who had turned off location-sharing settings.[[437]](#cite_note-Sonnemaker2021-438) In 2020, the [FBI](/wiki/FBI "FBI") used a [geofence warrant](/wiki/Geofence_warrant "Geofence warrant") to request data from Google about Android devices near the Seattle Police Officers Guild building following an arson attempt during Black Lives Matter protests. Google provided anonymized location data from devices in the area, which raised privacy concerns due to the potential inclusion of unrelated protesters.[[438]](#cite_note-439)

#### Copyright infringement

On March 20, 2024, Google was fined approximately $270 million by French regulators for using content from news outlets in France without proper disclosure to train its AI, Bard, now renamed Gemini, violating a previous commitment to negotiate content use transparently and fairly.[[439]](#cite_note-440)

#### U.S. government contracts

Following media reports about [PRISM](/wiki/PRISM_(surveillance_program) "PRISM (surveillance program)"), the [NSA](/wiki/National_Security_Agency "National Security Agency")'s massive electronic [surveillance program](/wiki/Mass_surveillance "Mass surveillance"), in June 2013, several technology companies were identified as participants, including Google.[[440]](#cite_note-441) According to unnamed sources, Google joined the PRISM program in 2009, as [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube") in 2010.[[441]](#cite_note-442) Google has worked with the [U.S. Department of Defense](/wiki/U.S._Department_of_Defense "U.S. Department of Defense") (DoD) on drone software through the 2017 [Project Maven](/wiki/Project_Maven "Project Maven") that could be used to improve the accuracy of [drone strikes](/wiki/Drone_strike "Drone strike").[[442]](#cite_note-443) In April 2018, thousands of Google employees, including senior engineers, signed a letter urging Google CEO Sundar Pichai to end this controversial contract with [the Pentagon](/wiki/The_Pentagon "The Pentagon").[[443]](#cite_note-444) Google ultimately decided not to renew this DoD contract, which was set to expire in 2019.[[444]](#cite_note-445) In 2022 Google shared a $9 billion contract from the Pentagon for cloud computing with Amazon, Microsoft, and Oracle.[[445]](#cite_note-446)

#### Google Nest hidden microphone incident

Further information: [Google Nest](/wiki/Google_Nest "Google Nest")

In February 2019, a [privacy](/wiki/Digital_privacy "Digital privacy") incident involving the Google Nest Guard system went public. The controversy stemmed from the fact that Nest Guard, a security device that was part of the Nest Secure system, contained a hidden microphone that was not disclosed in any product specifications. It resulted in a [public relations](/wiki/Public_relations "Public relations") failure.[[446]](#cite_note-:02-447)
